225838818.review:
This game is possibly the best sequel to the original file "Alien".   It perfectly captures the sense of dread, the atmosphere and the 70s Sci-Fi aesthetics, without seeming anachronistic.   The idea of the Alien being an indestructible being that actively hunts you, while also learning from your hiding habits, especially adds to the terror.   And the expansion of the lore (the lore from the original film) adds to the immersion.

The graphics hold up very well today (2026), particularly the use of lighting.  The environmental sound effects are the best I've heard in any video game... if you have the chance to play this in full 7.1 surround sound, I'd strongly recommend it.

Some have criticized the game for being too long.  I can see that argument, but I feel it simply means you're getting more game for your money, and I very much enjoyed every moment exploring the various environments.   If you're looking for something more like Aliens (the James Cameron sequel), then this possibly won't be to your taste.   It's a relatively slow paced stealth action game in which careful planning pays off.  There isn't really any first person shooting action to speak of.

225770794.review:
Probably the best game based on Alien. I wish I had completed it back in 2015, but it still holds up remarkably well, especially in its audio design, with sounds and countless small details either taken directly from the movie or faithfully recreated. They even brought back several original 1979 Alien cast members for the DLC, which adds even more authenticity.

The survival horror aspect is excellent — honestly better than in many newer horror games. The constant tension, sound design, and unpredictable Alien AI still make the experience genuinely stressful in the best possible way.

It does feel outdated in several areas, mostly visually and in some gameplay mechanics, but the incredibly faithful recreation of the movie’s atmosphere easily outweighs those drawbacks. Few licensed games capture the spirit of their source material this well.

Looking forward to part 2.

225413487.review:
As someone who doesn't get affected by what horror games usually try to put the player through, Alien: Isolation held me in tense for the first few hours for sure. The "horror" aspect in this game relies heavily on listening to the sounds, stealth-based gameplay, and atmosphere. You won't find tons of jumpscares here just for the sake of jumpscares.
I started my first playthrough with no knowledge about the plot pacing, and I only knew a little about the mechanics, so basically, my expectations were unaffected by outside info.
As an Alien universe fan - this is a dope game. The main plot is not that great, and I wish it had been worked on more. The game tends to repeat the first movie a lot, in some cases step-by-step, but is that a bad thing? For the game itself, I don't think so. It really gives you the experience of the characters in the original source, and for people who don't like the movie because it's old, confusing, fast-paced, etc. - this game is a perfect solution. 
The Xenomorph is a whole another topic - its AI is cool as heck. The game came out in 2014, and for that time it was done amazingly. You can learn Xeno behavior patterns and tactics pretty fast, but it still manages to catch you by surprise even many hours in, especially if you're playing on medium and higher difficulties.
The gameplay gets repetitive after a while, especially if you're planning on getting all achievements and playing Isolation multiple times, and the special maps don't really solve that problem. And I doubt that the available DLCs will. But other than that - I don't really have anything negative to say.
What I absolutely have to say is that this game has great optimization - I have a lot of problems with many games because of my old laptop, but with Isolation, never ever have I encountered FPS drops, freezes, or other ♥♥♥♥. The graphics aren't poor either, and the lighting in the game is really beautiful.
Overall, I highly recommend trying this out if you're tired of jumpscare slop, love sci-fi, or are just a fan of Alien

225169731.review:
Minor spoilers

6/10
Pros:
- The alien feels like a unique enemy in the genre, one that doesn't stick to any schedules and feels far more threatening than any else in the game.
- Retro future design is immaculate. Consistent design that feels authentic.
- Sound design does a great job of enhanching both the setting and tension.
- No performance issues (which should be the case for a 12 year old game on a 4070) and very rare visual bugs, I can only recall two times in the entire playthough
Cons:
- I feel like the "alien learns your behavior" is overblown. The only time that my approach changed was after I got the flamethrower since its easy to use it scare off the alien, so if anything, I felt less threatened by it as the game went on. It never learns to not be afraid of the flamethrower. There's other tools like the noisemaker or flashbang to distract it, but they never felt more effective than sneaking since the alien is always on the move; I never felt like I had to make it move.
- The non-alien enemies are not very threatening. The androids are slow, only every power-walking at you. You can literally just out-walk them and since much of the map is non-linear, you can usually lose them if you don't have the weapons to fight. The human enemies are dumb as rocks. On multiple occasions, I simply crouched next to them and they didn't see me and walked right past. 
- Multiple side characters die during the game, but with maybe one exception, you never really get a chance to grow attached, so while Ripley is upset, I didn't really care.
- Not a fan of the ending

Despite not loving this game, I'd be lying if I said I didn't enjoy it as a whole. There was enough here that I'd still recommend it to someone interested in the franchise or the genre. There's enough here and the problems are fixable enough that I'd be willing to play the recently announced sequel, just maybe not at full price.

225164940.review: It is no wonder that how good a horror video game is is often measured by how well it manages to keep up the pressure on you. Spamming the player with jumpscares soon becomes tedious and predictable—despite possibly managing to scare a couple of times on first playthroughs—and, after all, produces a rather artificial kind of fear,  thus failing to leave a lasting impression. Arguably, it is essentially the psychological pressure that makes a horror experience memorable, be it a video game, a movie or a book. And this is, undoubtedly, what [i]Alien: Isolation[/i] excels at best. A great addition to the excellent [i]Alien[/i] franchise and an undisputed sci-fi horror classic on its own.

225113701.review:
## A Masterpiece of Survival Horror: Alien: Isolation
**Alien: Isolation** isn't just a game; it’s a grueling, terrifying, and masterfully crafted love letter to Ridley Scott’s 1979 cinematic masterpiece. Even years after its release, it remains the gold standard for how to adapt a legendary film franchise into an interactive experience.
### Visual and Atmospheric Brilliance: The Giger Legacy
From the moment you step onto the Sevastopol Station, the **visual polish** is staggering. While H.R. Giger is widely praised for his grotesque, sexualized, and iconic creature designs, his genius extended far beyond the Xenomorph itself. He was instrumental in designing the original film's environments, and the game honors this by perfectly capturing that **asphyxiating, claustrophobic vibe**.
The "lo-fi" sci-fi aesthetic—chunky buttons and dark, steam-filled corridors—replicates Giger’s unique vision where technology and biology feel uncomfortably intertwined. These tight spaces are essential to the gameplay; they create a constant, looming feeling of having **nowhere to run**, making the environment as much of an enemy as the creature itself.
### The Genius of the Predator AI
The adrenaline rush of being hunted is priceless thanks to a brilliant **dual AI system**. One "Director AI" always knows your exact coordinates and constantly feeds hints to the Alien to nudge it toward your general vicinity. Meanwhile, the Alien’s own AI utilizes a sophisticated set of senses:
 * **Vision:** It uses three distinct vision cones—a long but narrow one for distance, an intermediate one, and a very short but wide cone to prevent you from simply sneaking by its side.
 * **Sound & Touch:** It reacts dynamically to noises (whether intentional distractions or player mistakes) and possesses a touch sensor that triggers instantly if you accidentally bump into it.
 * **Environmental Interaction:** The depth of play is incredible; for instance, you can manually disable a sector's air purification system to create a thick fog, significantly reducing the creature's line of sight to facilitate a desperate escape.
### Pacing and Endurance
If there is one small caveat, it’s that the journey can feel **a tad too long** toward the final act. Yet, paradoxically, shortening it doesn't feel like the right answer either. There is something about that grueling length that mirrors the protagonist's exhaustion; while it's hard to put a finger on exactly how to improve the pacing, the sheer density of the experience is part of what makes it so memorable. Every time you manage to evade the creature, the sense of **accomplishment and pure relief** is incredibly satisfying.
### Technical Excellence and Optimization
It’s also worth noting the game’s **exceptional optimization**. Even at its launch back in 2014, modest PCs were able to run the game on High settings with a very stable frame rate. It remains a benchmark for how to deliver top-tier graphics without alienating players with mid-range hardware.
### The Ultimate VR Experience
For those looking for the "cherry on top," you absolutely must try the **MotherVR** mod, especially when combined with the **Grand Mother** mod enhancements. The level of immersion provided by Virtual Reality is simply indescribable; the atmosphere becomes physically palpable and the sense of scale is terrifying.
 * **Current State:** It already supports **6DOF** with motion controls.
 * **Pro Tip:** While the developers have promised room-scale movement in the future, it is highly recommended to **play it seated** for now to ensure the best experience.
**Final Verdict:** With its flawless graphics, haunting sound design, tense gameplay, and brilliant optimization, *Alien: Isolation* creates an **unmatched, thick atmosphere of dread**. It is a definitive experience that every horror-loving gamer should play at least once. It is, quite simply, survival horror at its peak.

225039431.review:
The alien AI is okay and can lead to tense and fun situations, but the design of the alien is not elaborate enough to support the 15-20 hours of gameplay required to finish the game. So the developers pad out the game with less interesting enemies and a lot of chores where you run around and do mini-games. In many cases there are entire rooms designed where you do nothing but go through a sequence of interactions and minigames that lacks any stimulating element for the player whatsoever. I understand that the game needs contrast between intense moments, but it's often outright boring. I often found myself going through a third of a mission and asking myself "what was the point of that?". Entire segments that feel like they do not enrich the game in any way.

If you really like survival horror you can give this a try, but the game is overall deeply mediocre. The game plays quite well when the developers decide to give you fewer save points and larger maps where you can navigate around the alien more -- I would say this describes less than 20% of the game. The rest of the game is (1) chore simulator, (2) cinematics where you're allowed to move around but not meaningfully interact with the game, (3) android avoider, and (4) dealing with the alien in really cramped levels but the save station was 3 minutes ago so there's no real tension.

I would also not call it an imsim. There are many situations where the environment changes purely for artificial plot reasons. A door will open just because a character stopped talking even though there was no particular reason for the door to open. You revisit levels, but this is not utilized in any smart way, and there is usually no reason to go off the beaten path. You mostly just find yourself back at a level you were but now you have a higher power torch so you open a room and get some goodies -- it's basic stuff. I'm trying to say that the game has some of the hallmarks of an imsim, in the same way that "an RPG is a game where you level up", but its design is often at odds with what imsims are supposed to be.

Overall the levels are not interactable enough, the station does not run on any system in particular, there's little imsimmy about the game except the alien itself. You have less freedom in approaching a level than Thief gives you, for comparison.

224922348.review:
The deadliest thing in this game isn't the androids, the humans, or even the alien.
It's the jank.

Some examples:
-If you spray the xenomorph with the flamethrower to make it run away, sometimes it has to complete its "slow and intimidating walk" animation before it can transition to the "running" state, so you and the alien just awkwardly stare at each other for a moment (that feels like an eternity) wondering if the game is going to register that as a hit.
-Then, the alien rushes at you, and you think, "great, it didn't work" and spray more of your precious fuel, only for the alien to run past you and go into a vent because it was technically the closest one. Shouldn't it run AWAY from the fire? Oh well, I guess if it runs towards me after a spray, it's probably running away? No, wait, THAT time it killed me.
-Sometimes you spray the alien but it's already in its "grab" animation, so it just kills you. There's even a mechanic where if you do this, the alien is supposed to only damage you instead of insta-killing you (great idea!), but sometimes this just doesn't happen. (yes I had health)
-Several instances of the stun rod not connecting with androids when I clearly didn't miss and was in range. (yes it had ammo)
-Flinching from fire 5 ft away, forcing me to drop the gun I was aiming at an approaching enemy, causing my death.
-Spamming the "cancel" key to get away from a computer, only to die during the several seconds long animation of... turning away from the computer? WHICH YOU CAN DO WITH THE CAMERA WHILE THE ANIMATION PLAYS!?!?!

All of this and more pulls you out of the experience hard. I can't remember the last time I played a game that just didn't do what it was supposed to with such frequency. This is especially frustrating when these issues cause several deaths in a row. Don't get me wrong, I signed up to get killed by the alien, but not like this.

All that being said, the game is excellent when it works, which was roughly 80% of my run-through. The atmosphere is truly top notch. The ambient hum of the machinery while wandering through the dark perfectly encapsulates how it feels to move through your own home at night. I think this game outshines even PT in that regard, although it's been a while since I've played that. I just hope they can give the sequel more time in the oven. Worth it on sale.

Alien: Isolation isn't an action game—it's a study in primal fear. Creative Assembly nailed the core mechanic: you're defenseless against an unstoppable predator, forced to hide and survive through stealth alone. No combat, no shortcuts. This constraint is brilliant.
The asymmetrical AI keeps you genuinely tense. It learns, adapts, hunts relentlessly. The '70s retro-futurism nails the atmosphere without resorting to cheap scares.
If you value psychological tension over action, this is essential.

224825020.review:
I am happy that I finally completed this game as it was on my pile of shame for a long time. I am a fan of the survival horror genre, and I feel comfortable saying that this game belongs at the top. The game has no mercy for you. You have multiple lengthy encounters with the alien. The alien will act intelligently, and give you a really hard time. This probably explains why 89.2 percent of the players completed chapter 1 and only 19.9 percent of players have completed the game, so be warned: It is a very intense game which is suprisingly lengthy with about 20 hours of playing time. But it is an unique experience that you won't get anywhere else. 

You see that this project was created with love for the source material. Many of the sounds, music, graphics and 'engineering puzzles' are directly inspired from the Alien movies, with also multiple references to the stories of the old Alien movies. The level design is well done, and the story never gets stale as you try all that you can to survive. The game still looks great even though it is 12 years old, and as mentioned the Alien AI is nothing like any I have seen in other games. The alien will adapt, act differently and remain unpredictable throughout the game. I really love this game, and I am really happy that recently a sequel has been announced for this game. I hope it will be created with the same love as this game was created.

224793232.review:
It's heretical to say, but I just do not understand the hype this game gathered. 

I've owned it since it released, and I've redownloaded and restarted it multiple times because my taste in games and media has changed over the years, but I still cannot find any reason to keep playing. The Alien AI that had so much praise feels extremely simple and unrelenting in a "hide in this room for 5 minutes while the Alien constantly decides your room and the connecting hallway is the most interesting place to be", and it's crazy to be watching the Alien on the scanner to track it's movement and suddenly it teleports to a vent behind or above you; it doesn't feel persistent or make any logical sense in it's actions... The Alien feels like it's there to pad out the game time, and as someone who didn't grow up with the Alien franchise (and have nothing against it either), I just don't find any point in slogging through the game that makes me wait for another section to do the exact same thing; walk 5 feet, wait 5 minutes, walk 5 feet, wait 5 minutes...

I am a big fan of cassette/nasa-punk asthetics, so the most fun I had was oogling all the weird details and wishing I could push all the big clunky buttons.

224728315.review: I rarely feel compelled to take the time to write a review, even if I really enjoy a game. This is an exception. If you're a fan of survival horror, or just a big fan of the Alien films; this game is a masterpiece in my opinion. The attention to detail in the world building and art direction is amazing. The sound design is terrifying. The musical score is heart-poundingly intense and a beautiful homage to the original film's score.  The game doesn't pull any punches. The way the AI Director and the Xeno AI work together is unlike anything else I've ever experienced. It's a long, hard road, and I enjoyed every second of it. Even the frustrating bits. I would recommend Alien: Isolation to anyone.

224696180.review:
Alien: Isolation is a game designed around process rather than substance. The phenomenal environmental design is let down by shallow gameplay that consists of gathering keys, pressing buttons, following the quest arrow on your motion tracker, and muddling through mediocre combat and the same mini-games over and over. Ultimately it feels more like a barely-interactive movie than an actual game where the player has to make decisions. 

It's also insufferably annoying if you don't install the alien behavior mod, and the ending is bad. 

Given that barely 20% of players on steam ever get to the ending, it seems like most people make the sane choice of quietly filing this one away after a couple hours. 

Hats off to the artists, though. On max settings, the game still looks incredible 12 years later.

224508273.review:
This is probably the most stressed I’ve ever been playing a video game. Not “oh that was tense” stressed. I mean actual pause-the-game-because-my-heart-is-trying-to-escape-my-chest stressed.

There were multiple moments where I just had to stop because of how scared and stressed I was, sit there, and reassess my life choices.

The alien being completely unkillable is genius and also borderline cruel. In most horror games you eventually get to the point where you think “okay I can handle this now, I have a gun.”
Here? No. You have a gun... but no hope that it'll do anything

And it makes it way scarier. Because you’re not fighting it, youre just crawling along in the same space and praying it doesn't notice you.

Also, this game is long. Like really long. But that just adds to the stress. At some point it stops being “fun horror game” and turns into: “When is this nightmare actually going to end?”

And the worst part is… that feeling is exactly what makes it work.

10/10 would panic again.

224333495.review:
The Alien isn't smart, it cheats.

It always knows where you are regardless of any clue. The only thing that it "learns" is not to be fooled by the same decoy. "Oh, look how smart it is, if you hide too much on lockers, it will start looking inside the lockers, but if you spread your hiding spots, it will never check the locker..." Well, if it knows you been hiding on the lockers why doesn't it get you on the first place? The answer is simple: it isn't smart strategy, there is a system that tells it where you've been hiding. Also, it has "tight senses", it can hear the tiny beep of your radar, but it cannot hear you slamming the locker door shut!?

On the difficulty recommended by the developers, the alien takes a very long time to back off, so it will take even more time for you to be able to move from point A to point B. Summing this to the scarce saving system and this game isn't very suitable for adults.

With all that being said, the ambiance is well-made. If you have all the time in the world, you like a challenge, and you don't mind some occasional unfair deaths that will break your immersion, this game is actually good for you.

224315357.review:
[i]Difficulty Completed: Nightmare[/i]

The horror genre is unrecognizable from how it looked a decade ago, and that can be absolutely said for its presence in the gaming industry. Nowadays, much to its detriment, AAA studios are abandoning horror. This is because the average player began to prefer "fast-food" experiences, which has led to indie horror to dominate the space greatly. Stagnation and mediocrity soon followed, as many developers today aim only for low-budget, trendy spooks.

However, one game, now 12 years old at this point, always comes back to remind everyone on how horror is truly made. One that was incredibly innovative for its time, and still is remembered fondly by many fans of horror today. You're looking at it.

[b]Good Horror Things:[/b]
[list]
[*]While you can most certainly label this as a horror experience, it's also like a constant battle of the minds between the player and a primal but [i]very intelligent[/i] creature. What makes this so? The Alien has not one, but [b]two[/b] programmed brains. One active, the other passive. One actually knows where you are at ALL times, but it is not in control. It feeds hints to the second brain that is controlling the Alien's actions, to which the second acts on those hints to try and find you.

[*]What results is something that feels like a real, deductive monster, and not just a simple "stalker" like Mr. X from [i]Resident Evil[/i]. Not only that, but Creative Assembly has done a wonderful job in making additional behaviors for the Alien as well. For example: aim a flamethrower at it, and it will suddenly stop, slowly following and thinking on how to get to you without being scorched. Flame it, and it will retreat. [i]However:[/i] abuse the flamethrower, and it'll simply push through the pain to get you in the end. It's just [b]amazing[/b], and I wish more games did this. 

[*]I highly recommend playing this on Nightmare, as once you first meet the Alien, it will [b]never[/b] stop stalking or hunting you except for select parts. If you even run a few steps, that is enough to warrant a hunt just about anywhere. You have to be absolutely quiet at all times, be careful of where you step, and be mindful of the risks you take, especially since you will have little resources to work with on this difficulty. All of this, but while having a motion sensor that glitches and gives somewhat incorrect information, along with no hud elements.

[*]This game will be having you hold your breath in many areas. Nothing more intense than having to wait for the save station, or trying to start a generator, all while you hear the heavy footsteps of the alien nearby. I was seen, hearing the awful screech and fast thumps, only to run into the elevator and press a button. Those doors closed right in its face to where parts of the Alien clipped through the doors. Something that big should not run that fast.

[*]This game plays heavily on risk. Group of people who won't let you pass without shooting you? Well, you could distract them. Just know... you may attract something [b]much worse[/b] in doing so. Yeah, it may clear them out, but now it has good reason to look for you too.

[*]Come into a room full of armed people, and they'll be so cocksure and threatening, guns-drawn and heads held high as they hunt and shoot at you. Yeah, you can shoot back, or use distractions or the like. However, once that Alien drops out of the vents, the ENTIRE situation and atmosphere changes, because [i]it's quiet time[/i]. 

[*]Loud threats are now replaced with quiet sobbing as they run and hide. They can't shoot at you anymore, because that means certain death for them. I purposefully lured the Alien to me and some hostile survivors once, and as I was moving past a man that was hiding, he whispered "♥♥♥♥ you". Don't be such a negative-nancy and enjoy the little things, dude.

[*]The Alien just chooses whenever it wants to hunt. You could suddenly meet it, die, reload, and it just won't show up the next go around, and vice versa. This unpredictability in its behavior adds a sort of volatility to the gameplay, and enhances the experience.

[*]This game came out in 2014, but you wouldn't tell if you didn't know that (except for the character models). Baseline, this game holds up extremely well, but I also highly recommend the [i]Improved Graphics[/i] mod on Nexus Mods to improve them further with high resolution shadows, FOV slider, reflections controls, etc.

[*]Not only that, but the 80's Sci-FI style of the original Alien movies has been captured wonderfully here, and not one thing felt out of place.

[*]Also, this game is incredibly easy to run. You can even run it on your phone. No, I am not kidding. You really can. Check the app store.[/list]

[b]Some Caveats:[/b]
[list]
[*]There is a section of this game that decides to give you breathing room from the Alien, which is fine, but I feel it is the worst part as it's too long, all while just being a slog to get through. I wouldn't be surprised if the developers just decided to add this as padding far into production. You can trim 1-2 hours from this game and the overall pacing and story wouldn't be too affected.

[*]This game has an FPS bug as well. I won't count this towards its score, as this game did come out roughly 12 years ago, but it is an issue nonetheless. So if you run this game above 105FPS, the audio for when the Alien kills you bugs out to where it doesn't play at all. Not only that, but animations will play much faster at 360+FPS, so you have to choose between having proper death sounds or an even smoother experience. If you do lock your FPS, make sure to also keep VSync on, as I found this quite odd bug where the game actually went into slow-motion when I locked my FPS, and I believe its an issue of hard-setting it below your monitor's refresh rate. VSync keeps it at that refresh rate while you can lock your FPS at 105.

[*]There is also a soft-lock, but this is only an issue on Nightmare really. There is one part of the game (the last mission) to where you will be incredibly low on ammo, and have to crawl around in a vent with two facehuggers. Problem is that you can't melee in a vent, so if you don't have at least two bullets here, you will die. You cannot outcrawl them and its instant death when they get you. This means you have no way of progressing, so you have to reload the mission to get more ammo. Watch out for this.[/list]

[i]Alien: Isolation[/i] is the absolute [b]definitive[/b] horror experience, one of the best if not the best on the market today. If you are looking for a good, scary time, maybe just guide your attention away from [i]Poppy's Playtime[/i], yet another analogue horror, the millionth [i]FNAF[/i] clone, heckin' creepy liminal spacerinos, or just whatever nonsense is the trend for horror today, and treat yourself to this instead. You deserve it. 

Hope everyone is excited for the upcoming sequel. I sure am.

[i]Alien Isolation: [b]9.5/10[/b][/i]

224280973.review:
O Legado Técnico de Alien: Isolation

Alien: Isolation consolidou-se como um divisor de águas no survival horror através de três pilares fundamentais de design:

    IA de Duas Camadas: A arquitetura do jogo utiliza um sistema "Diretor" (que monitora o fluxo) aliado a uma IA de Xenomorfo que aprende e reage aos hábitos do jogador. Isso eliminou comportamentos scriptados, criando um predador inteligente e genuinamente imprevisível.

    Imersão Diegética: A ausência de interfaces (HUD) tradicionais na tela, substituídas por dispositivos físicos no mundo do jogo (como o rastreador e terminais), força o jogador a gerenciar riscos em tempo real, sem pausas artificiais.

    Estética "Lo-Fi Sci-Fi": O uso técnico de grãos, aberração cromática e design de áudio dinâmico emulou com perfeição a atmosfera de 1979, garantindo uma coesão visual que envelhece melhor do que muitas tentativas de fotorrealismo puro.

Legado e Influência: O jogo estabeleceu o padrão moderno para o chamado "Terror de Evasão". Títulos contemporâneos, como os remakes de Resident Evil e Routine, herdaram o DNA de Isolation ao priorizar a perseguição constante por inimigos persistentes, a escassez rigorosa de recursos e a interação ambiental. O sucesso do título provou, técnica e racionalmente, que restringir o poder do jogador é a ferramenta mais eficaz para gerar tensão e imersão.

224190296.review:
I played this game almost exclusively on the Steam Deck OLED. This game was praised back when it first came out for having (as Mandalore Gaming put it) "wicked good optimization." It was able to run smoothly on potato hardware even back in 2014. So you can bet it runs well now--on the Steam Deck OLED I was able to play at 90 FPS, and for a game this visually impressive, that's quite the achievement. It's a gorgeous-looking game even by today's standards, with the only exception being the facial animation / models. The ship, effects, and alien itself are all eye candy.

It's definitely best on controller--I dabbled with using mouse and keyboard and it's clearly a controller-first experience, making it great for Steam Deck. The music and atmosphere is some of the best in the gaming industry; you can tell the developers put their soul into making the environments actually feel like the 1970s film.

One thing I rarely hear anyone bring up is the level design; the Xenomorph has excellent AI behavior which makes it feel like it's actually hunting you (well, most of the time--there are times where it doesn't see you when it should), and it's unpredictable which keeps the gameplay loop engaging. But I would argue that the level design (especially for the hospital chapters) is a big reason the core gameplay loop works so well. Things like lockers, beds, and tables for hiding feel well-placed. You can tell they tested the gameplay to try to make it challenging without feeling unfair. The emergency telephone stations ("save" stations) feel well-spaced out as well most of the time. In some really difficult areas you'll encounter two save stations close to each other, but in most areas the save stations are just far enough apart that making it to the next one feels like a small achievement. I would put the save station placement next to something like the bonfire placement in Dark Souls 1, where reaching a new bonfire is a big relief.

A lot of people complain about the story being too long or dragging on too much, and I empathize with that without necessarily agreeing. If it was a book or movie, then yes, I would say that the story definitely drags, but this isn't a story-driven game, it's essentially a hide and seek gameplay-focused game. The story is merely a vehicle to give you good gameplay, so the story being long is a good thing if you find yourself enjoying the game and don't want it to end. I played it on the "hard" difficulty for my first playthrough, which was the game recommends. That's what I recommend too. I've played it on medium and nightmare as well, and what I found was that medium feels too easy because the Xeno wanders around a lot more, and nightmare makes the Xeno a lot of fun but makes the Working Joe androids extremely tedious. It's not so much that the androids become much more tanky, it's also that nightmare difficulty makes resources almost non-existent, so you don't have any ammo or stun charges to use against the androids. I get that it's supposed to be the ultimate challenge, but it would have been nice for there to be a middle option that makes the enemies tougher than "hard" but doesn't limit the resources. Maybe like a "very hard" difficulty option between hard and nightmare. I'd say "hard" is the most fun difficulty level to play on, because the Xenomorph is really smart but the resource scarcity and android difficulty isn't super tedious.

I haven't finished all the DLC, but from what I've played they're fun little add ons. Don't expect any story content, the purpose of the DLC is just to give you arcade-like challenges facing off against the alien. It's great for introducing friends to the game without them having to go through the first couple hours of the main story.

Alien Isolation is not perfect--the story drags, the characters are pretty basic (though I love Amanda's voice acting), but the gameplay and atmosphere are second to none. Isolation is a flawed masterpiece, I give it a 9/10. I'd check out Mandalore's video or Beam Buddy's longer deep-dive if you want to learn more.

223337214.review:
* I am currently playing the game on Medium difficulty and full settings (motion blur off) *

[h1] Overview [/h1]

Very tense and atmospheric survival horror, faithfully adapted from the Alien franchise. If you like resource scarcity, strategizing and stealth/combat avoidance, you'll love this game.

If you're a fan of the Alien franchise, as I'm sure most who will play this game are, you'll be happy to find a very true-to-source representation of that universe here. A great deal of the enjoyment I get from this game is seeing the aesthetics I love from this franchise - the setting, gear and, of course, the Alien (far from the only thing threatening you in this game) are all wonderfully realized here.

[h2] Graphics [/h2]

The graphics hold up very nicely for a nearly 12 year old game, though there are a several mods available if you're looking to spruce it up a little (including the obligatory "make the main enemy Thomas the Tank Engine" mod). I'm running the "Improved Graphics" mod from Nexus, and I've encountered some blocking with contrasting light levels if they're passing through fog, though that's been my biggest complaint thus far. I cannot say for certain that this occurs without this mod installed.

[h2] Story [/h2]

The story is engaging and, as far as I know, canon to the movie franchise. Supposedly, this game partially inspired the story of Alien: Romulus. Take from that what you will. I think the opening "hook" ( [spoiler] you play as Ripley's daughter, who is trying to find out what happened to her mother [/spoiler] ) is a good one and has me curious where the game will go, and especially curious about the story of the coming sequel.

[h2] Gameplay [/h2]

Enemies are quite the obstacle as you are not a Colonial Marine in this game. Combat resources are scarce and most enemies I have encountered are spongy. Especially of note - the Xenomorph cannot be killed. You will have to find ways to distract, deter, or avoid it altogether.

The AI will learn aspects of your gameplay and adapt to it, so you will have to do the same. It keeps the 
game interesting, since you'll need to formulate new strategies every so often to sneak past or escape from enemies.

There is a variety of tools and weapons that allow you to try different strategies to advance through the game, and you'll be needing them. Several types of puzzles, all fairly simple, occur frequently and are satisfying enough, though have yet to be challenging. Hiding spots are available but sparing enough to keep it from being too easy to escape.

[h2] Performance [/h2]

The game performs pretty well, though if left running in the background for too long it usually takes on a slow-motion or low-gravity effect, which in my experience has required me to quit to the main menu and reload to fix. Due to this I recommend saving before taking a break if you plan to leave the game open while away. I've yet to encounter an enemy while this is happening, so I'm not sure the effect applies to everything in the game. Better safe than sorry.

[h2] Review Is Incomplete [/h2]

This is a review in progress as I only have a few hours on this playthrough and I'm already further than I was with more than double those hours in my previous attempt. I have not completed this game. As surface level as this may be, I hope it helps someone decide that this game is for them, or saves someone of the opposite persuasion the time and money this game requires.

222986482.review:
Alien: Isolation

this game… man. it doesn’t try to entertain you the way most games do. it tries to make you uncomfortable, and it’s *really* good at it.

you don’t feel like a hero in this. you feel like prey.

the alien isn’t some scripted boss you learn and beat. it feels like it’s actually hunting you. you’ll hide, hold your breath, watch that motion tracker… and still get caught because it *learns*. that’s the part that messes with your head. you stop trusting your own habits because the game punishes patterns

half the time you’re not even playing, you’re just sitting there in a locker like
“don’t move… don’t breathe… don’t make a sound”
and somehow that’s more intense than any action game

the atmosphere carries hard too. everything feels old, worn, and real. like you’re actually stuck in that universe. the sounds alone will get you—metal creaks, distant footsteps, vents… you start hearing things that might not even be there

it’s slow though. and I mean *slow*. if you’re not in the mood for tension, it can feel like it drags. and yeah, it can get frustrating when you die to something that feels out of your control

but when it works… it really works

this isn’t a “fun” game in the usual sense. it’s stress, tension, and that constant feeling that something is right behind you

and somehow… that’s exactly why it’s so good

---

### quick take

**rating:** 9/10
**genre:** survival horror / stealth

**pros**

* alien AI feels unpredictable and smart
* insane atmosphere and sound design
* constant tension that actually sticks with you
* stays true to the alien universe

**cons**

* pacing can feel slow
* can be frustrating at times
* not something you can just casually play

---

### final thought

if you want a horror game that actually makes you feel hunted instead of powerful, this is it. just don’t expect to relax while playing it… because you won’t 😏

222616773.review:
I just finished Alien Isolation on the Steam Deck and I played on Nightmare difficulty for my first playthrough, and what an incredible game man.

Its unreal. Every sound matters, every step feels terrifying and the Xenomorph is genuinely unpredictable. Going in blind on the hardest difficulty made it even better no hand holding, just pure survival horror at its best.

I actually managed to avoid killing any humans which was very difficult to do and i only died once I was so close to that no death achievement but honestly that just made the run amazing. The game rewards patience, awareness, and staying relaxed while under pressure.

And if you know what you’re doing in Nightmare difficulty it almost stops feeling like a typical horror game and starts feeling more like a space station maintenance simulator carefully moving through systems, managing resources, and solving problems under constant pressure.

I just want to give quick advice for the Xenomorph sections just walk, don’t run too much. Staying calm and controlled makes a big difference.

222418228.review:
Alien: Isolation is easily one of the best horror games I’ve ever played. Instead of relying on cheap jumpscares every five seconds, it builds constant tension and makes you feel hunted the entire time. The atmosphere is insane, the sound design is top tier, and the Sevastopol station feels so detailed and alive in the worst possible way. Everything about it just drips with dread.

The alien itself is what really makes the game special. It doesn’t just feel like some scripted enemy, it feels smart, unpredictable, and genuinely terrifying. You’re never fully comfortable, because even when things seem quiet, you know it could all go wrong in seconds. That kind of pressure makes every little objective feel intense.

What I also love is how faithful it is to the original Alien movie. The retro-futuristic style, the lighting, the technology, the overall vibe, it all feels like a playable version of that world. Even after all these years, it still looks and sounds incredible.

It’s not a perfect game, and some parts can drag a little, but when it’s at its best, Alien: Isolation is a masterclass in survival horror. It’s stressful, immersive, and unforgettable.

221832200.review:
Absolutely fabulous game, I loved it. The devs clearly understood what made the first Alien movie an all-timer and recreated it perfectly in this game. Played it a first time, then a second to complete all the achievements and still wanted to play more after that.

The graphics are good and aged pretty well for a decade old game, although the age shows a little bit on character models. Other than that, the environment is beautiful.

As I said earlier, the game is faithful to the first movie. I really enjoyed the story and its twists. I would say the horror aspect is definitely present, although I'm not very recepteive to horror in games/movies so it's harder for me to tell. There's also lore everywhere, notes to read to better understand what happened on the space station and I had a great time searching and reading everything. And there's even a twitter account easter egg if you manage to find it!

The gameplay itself is fun as well, you've got a good array of different weapons and different items to use. The AI of the xenomorph is great, especially if you play on harder difficulties, while still not being hardcore and allowing more casual players to enjoy the game as well.

Only downsides are the controls that felt a little bit weird at times and it took me a while to get used to them. And also the DLCs weren't that intersting in my opinion, but that's secondary.

The price is reasonable, especially on sale when you can get a huge discount so I highly recommend this game!

220802329.review:
[h1]The Masterpeice That Overstayed Its Welcome[/h1]

Alien: Isolation is an immersive and beautiful installment in the Alien franchise.  It faithfully renders the Alien universe founded in 1979 by Ridley Scott.  Isolation also wonderfully expands on the Alien universe in many ways, not least by introducing the famous Weyland-Yutani's industry competitor: the ailing Seegson Corporation.  Seegson Synthetics is a manufacturer of rudimentary androids, located aboard Sevastapol Station orbiting gas giant KG348.

[olist]
	[*]Alien Isolation's main story takes place in real time.  This deeply grounds the game's pacing and level design aboard the sprawling Sevastapol Station.  You will cover miles of station facilities, crawl through maintenance tunnels, and even take a few impressive strolls on exterior gangways.
	[*]Alien Isolation's main antagonist, The Alien, is a dynamic and ruthless hunter that will pursue you throughout the station.  The alien will prey upon your habits, expanding its behaviors to match your fallbacks.  If you hide in lockers, it will start sniffing them out.  If you crawl into every vent, it will eventually follow you in there.  If you attack it, it will become more aggressive.
[/olist]

Both of these features are Alien: Isolation's biggest strengths, but together result in its biggest weakness.  After the first dozen or so hours, the story looks to be drawing near a close- only to drag on for about 7-10 more hours of grueling and underwhelming gameplay.  At this point, the novelty of the Alien's "dynamic" AI begins to wear off, until it becomes a cheap teleporting nuisance.

The tension is palpable, and never have I ever screamed out loud at a game so many times.  While it makes for a truly thrilling experience- the Alien's unpredictability diminishes the power of player choice, with death and reload often resulting in an easier outcome due to behavioral RNG.  Per the game's recommendation, I beat the game on the hardest difficulty.  I would not recommend this to most players.  Most report this to be a 20 hour game, but it easily became 30 for me.

There is an infamously difficult mission (5), which was so hard that I simply stepped away from the game for about 14 months.  There is a point early on in the game that seems to allow you to trap the creature in a room by disabling certain doors- but a quick google search reveals that doing so will just cause it to respawn outside the room and continue its pursuit.  The developer's claim that [i]"there are only 2 moments in which the Alien is teleported to the player"[/i], is categorically false.

Isolation's third act is an incessant clinging to the player's time and attention which almost ruins the whole experience.  If it weren't for the consistently beautiful set pieces, I would dare to not recommend this game.  Despite the ending dragging on for hours of gameplay, the finale is underwhelming and shallow.  I have not played the DLC's, and I doubt I ever will.  I appreciate Creative Assembly's adherence to authenticity and realistic pacing, as well as their desire to provide an abundant experience.  But this dish is ultimately overcooked, almost burnt.  

[h3]Player beware.[/h3]

I don't tend to align with IGN's farcical pedantics, but [url=https://www.ign.com/articles/2014/10/03/alien-isolation-review]Ryan McCaffrey's review absolutely nailed it.[/url]

Side Note: I played this game entirely on a Steam Deck with an Xbox Controller, running through a television at 1080p at full quality- the game is wonderfully optimized- and still looks absolutely amazing 12 years later.

220620988.review:
[h1] Why did I wait so long...?[/h1]

I'm kicking myself for waiting so long to truly experience this game. I've owned it for around 10 years, but after playing only 20 minutes, I set it aside, misguided by misconceptions about its gameplay. I thought it would be a bland, unengaging hide-and-seek experience – a gross underestimation, as I've discovered.

Having now completed the game on the hardest difficulty, I can confidently say it's become one of my all-time favorites. The atmosphere, world-building, story, and gameplay converge seamlessly, immersing me in an alien movie. The graphics, despite being nearly 12 years old, have aged remarkably well.

The gameplay, in particular, is a masterclass in design. Once it clicked, I was hooked, I could not put the game down. I would 100% recommend this game to anyone who loves the Alien franchise and survival horror games in general, this is one of the best, and I'm eagerly awaiting the sequel.

220475320.review:
I don't often play horror games, but I decided to give Alien a try. And of course, I decided to try my hand at the recommended difficulty right away, which is hard. And I have to say, the game keeps you on your toes the whole time. I didn't read anything about the game to avoid spoilers. I also didn't know the game's specifics.

At the beginning, only the androids get in your way, and you think it's not that hard. But then you start hiding with the Alien, and I realized the game is testing your patience. Unfortunately, I wasn't so patient XD. I ended up finishing the game on the easy level, just to enjoy it. The game is worth playing and getting your nerves going. The puzzles are simple. I just wish the map was better. Perhaps they made it to match the style of the film, but it wasn't always clear which floor the target was on.

You can't hide for long on the hard difficulty; the Alien starts checking all the cabinets, and you end up using up all your health just holding your breath. The game forces you to go out and "flank" the Alien from different angles. It definitely got easier when they gave you a flamethrower. The scariest moments are when facehuggers jump on your face XD; for me, that was scarier than the Alien.
I'd give the game an 8/10. I recommend it.

220135306.review: Alien: Isolation is a true love letter to its source material. Creative Assembly knew exactly what they were doing when making this game and that's why 12 years later it is still one of the most revered horror games in gaming history. The graphics are simply insane for 2014. The Alien AI is great and alive. At first, it was very hard as I learned how to survive, throughout its duration the horror element was reduced but never erased. The story and environmental storytelling was great and has clear respect for the source material. That being said, the game is not without flaws. The game was simply too long, about halfway through I was ready for the ride to be over. Its hard to endure such a high stress environment for such a extended duration of time. It took me two completely separate play-sessions separated by months to finish the base game. The DLC is also petty strong and presents some unique scenarios for those enjoying the base game, I personally love the high stakes and challenge of the survivor modes, although they may be a tad too hard. I would easily recommend this game to anyone who enjoys Ridley Scott's 1979 masterpiece as this is a perfect companion piece. There is a lot of game here for a fair price.

219890848.review:
Honestly one of the only horror survival titles I can safely say I absolutely adore.
The atmosphere is just unfriendly, futuristic and cold with a lot of metallic and dark colors, the enemies combined with the soundtrack create an extreme sense of unease, the intelligence of the xenomorph keeps you on your toes.

But most of all: the game is FUN. It makes you feel intelligent for beating levels by using the exact thing humans have used for millennia to come out on top: Our ingenuity. Using devices to attract the alien to dispatch other enemies to conserve ammo, using flashbangs or other types of bombs to disrupt tankier foes so you can get past safely without risking damage or other more precious resources, redirecting power in ways that benefit you best in that very moment...

Safe to say, that even though I haven't FULLY completed the game yet, I will be fully completing it by getting every achievement and beating Nightmare difficulty. Wish me luck, and if you ever get the opportunity to? Buy this game. You won't regret it.

THE Alien experience. Very nice and is well-optimised to run very smoothly, the only glitchy bit I ever experienced was when using a ladder sometimes. It might just be a bug with animation though, it still executes fine in any case. You should play on Hard, it felt to me to be just the right amount of difficultly except for 2 parts, which were extremly tedious and I kept dying, but I guess I could have been less stingy with my tools in retrospect. There is a disturbing lack of cute cat, however, especialy with how much importance is placed on Jonesy in the first 2 films. Big sad.

Keep moving but stay quiet, as the Alien will linger in your area otherwise. Don't run, unless you have just scared it off as it will return to that point if you are still there. Also, vary up your hiding spots/tools or try to hide not in something, as the Alien learns your hiding spots the more you use them as long as it doesn't lead to your death. It also learns about your tools and they become less effective, like learning to not be afraid of fire to kill you.

Great game! 

DLC: Crew Expendable and Last survivor take place at the start and end of the original movie, with voices from the original movie except for Ash. Only 20-30 minutes long each at the higher difficulties, but both are still good fun, getting to explore the Nostromo in game form. 
There are a few other 'dlc' but they are mostly just time trials or survival missions, with no new voice-acting.

218890255.review:
I finally got around to playing Alien: Isolation, and WOW — it lived up to the hype. If you’re a fan of survival horror or the Alien universe, this is one of the best experiences out there.

From the moment you start, the game masterfully captures the tense, slow-burn terror of the original Alien movies. The atmosphere is chef’s kiss — dark, eerie, and constantly unsettling. Everything from the lighting to the audio design works together to keep you on edge. Just hearing that distant scream or footsteps creeping around a corner instantly put me into full-on paranoid mode.

The real star here is the Xenomorph. It doesn’t follow set paths or predictable patterns, which means every encounter feels different and genuinely frightening. You’re almost always outmatched — hiding, escaping, or outsmarting the alien instead of just shooting it — and that makes every narrow escape feel like a huge win.

If you love atmospheric horror that prioritizes fear and survival over mindless combat, this is a must-play. A solid 9/10 from me — scary, smart, and unforgettable.

I really wanted to like this game. I gave it a fair chance and played roughly halfway through, but I just don’t have the motivation to continue.

I know a lot of people praise this game, and I can understand why — the atmosphere is well done and the presentation is strong. But for me, it just never clicked.

The Alien simply wasn’t scary. After a while, the gameplay loop started to feel repetitive: constantly looking for vents to hide in, searching for alternate routes, hacking terminals, and running away. Instead of tension, I mostly felt frustration and exhaustion. It felt less like survival horror and more like endlessly avoiding the same threat in the same ways.

I respect what the game tries to do, but unfortunately it just didn’t work for me. I’m stopping here and leaving a negative review because, despite giving it enough time, I didn’t enjoy the experience.

Maybe it’s just not my type of horror game. :(

217935532.review:
Even though this game easily remains one of the undisputed best horror games ever made after all those years, I honestly don't recommend it to anyone, even to those who are hardcore horror fans. This is not your average run and gun, zombies, monsters, haunted environment bla bla bla game. It's extremely stressful and it was programmed to be unpredictable no matter the difficulty you play this game on, It's absolutely terrifying because the Alien can pop out at any time and all you hear is its footsteps knowing if you make any noise or a single wrong move, you're finished.

The Alien will fool you as if it didn't see you, then jumps on you when you don't expect it. It'll bait you into its traps, and you can hear it hissing, growling, and screeching all over the place. It'll adapt to your tactics and learn fast how to counter your playstyle. Even the environment itself is very tense, for example the doors sound effects are very close to the Alien's sounds so you'd think it's behind you or near you.

Alien Isolation plays mind games with you all the time, and you have to be alert with nerves of steel otherwise pick something else.

217676438.review:
One of the best horror games ever made.

I’ve beaten it a few times on console and bought it on pc recently, every playthrough that I have done have always been enjoyable.

The graphics look great for a game that came out in 2014, and the retro-futuristic aesthetic perfectly matches the original Alien movie, and if you're into such aesthetic you will end up loving it, each level looks amazing but some levels were just simply stunning to look at.

The story and characters are well written and it fits nicely between the first two films. Nothing that big to the point it's groundbreaking, but it fits enough that is respectful to the Alien franchise. It even seals up a plothole from the movie Aliens which I wont say (spoiler reasons).

The soundtrack itself is absolutely amazing. Many of the tracks feel like a they could fit right in on the original films, and if you loved those then you will most definitely love this score, especially the scores during the last few missions.

The gameplay is amazing as a survival horror game. Most tools are useful besides some of them being heavy on items in order to craft them but are still useful nonetheless, the weapons you get are useful against the androids and humans, but the xenomorph unlike the other threats is a complete monster that you can only scare away temporarily, while he isn't always around if you're loud enough he will show up. Nothing beats the feeling of being hunted for most of the game by the xenomorph forcing you to be careful not making much noise or being mindful with how you use your gadgets. The stealth is great, and the AI for the xenomorph is so well designed to make the player switch up their way of playing. The devs definetly put great work into the Ai.

The atmosphere is tense. The sound designs are well made whether it's the ambience, xenomorph, or the station itself, they're all amazing. Only game where the sounds make me hide for a long time inside a locker or under a table waiting for the xenomorp to make a sound in the vents just to be sure I am safe to go to the objective or move around.

Downsides:
I really didn't encounter anything bad besides a small bug or glitch? That didn't allowed me to save during the ending mission, but it got quickly fixed by restarting the game.

TL;DR
If you are into horror games and love the Alien franchise, then this game is a must for you. It’s a masterpiece that was made by people who clearly love the franchise, and did they're reasearch in order to keep it as loyal to the franchise. Truly had a great time playing this game.

217469393.review:
Excels in almost all aspects it sets out to. When looking at something within the Alien universe, the most important aspect for me is the horror elements of the first film and the atmosphere it sets up. For the most part, Isolation adheres to this. Right from the start, the atmosphere and mood are correct as you set upon the expansive space station Sevastopol. Playing as Amanda Ripley, daughter of Ellen Ripley from the film Sevastopol, is where the flight log for Ellen’s ship is, and Amanda is seeking answers. What follows is Amanda’s attempt to recover this log and eventually escape the station and deal with the Xenomorphs.

The design of Sevastopol has a more analog aesthetic to it, with many levers and buttons; some parts look ripped right out of the film.  Each level is intricately designed and connected with places to hide as well as vents for further navigation options. Throughout the story, you’ll visit these areas many times and see how they’ve changed, and more of the map is unlocked. Despite being over 10 years old as of writing, the art style holds up well with its semi-realistic look, greatly helped by the lighting, which feels appropriate for a rundown space station. Creating such an immersive world meant that I wanted to spend time in it, exploring it all and learning all I could about it, the additional log entries, and such tell a more whole picture of what happened to the station and some of the people you see or hear about. Levels go well with the main gameplay and provide many options for how to proceed with them.

Horror games usually manage to have great sound design, and Isolation is no exception. Here is one of the few games that sound feels so important as you listen for the Xenomorph’s walking, here it is in the vents, or here it is leaving. Androids and humans also have sounds to indicate where they are, and listening becomes extremely important to surviving. As mentioned, with the overall design of Sevastopol, the more analog focus allows for a great depth of sounds, each device has its own beeps and blips. Hearing the beeping of a save point brought a cathartic feeling after the intensity of what came before. Looking at the soundtrack, it mainly follows closely with the soundtrack from the film, although some of the more tense sequences feature more intense music, the film soundtrack was used somewhat sparingly only for some key moments of gameplay or discovery, while the tense sequences overstayed the welcome a bit, also with the importance of sound in this having music barring down when I was trying to listen became frustrating, especially as the volume was fine for most the of game. 

It is a first-person game, though the main focus is on walking around and staying stealthy, either through timing when to move or making use of vents and hiding spots. While there is some combat, it is not the major focus, and sometimes it is only a reprieve (especially from the Xenomorph). Combat aspects are traditional survival horror, limited supplies, and difficult-to-defeat enemies. Another aspect of the gameplay and combat is crafting, which allows you to make things to distract or even kill enemies and heal yourself. Initially, I didn’t use the crafting, but as the game progressed and the difficulty increased, I started using it more and found it useful. The worst aspect of the gameplay was the minigames and some of the controls. There is a rewire system, which involves allocating points to speakers(to distract enemies) and other such systems. This felt underused and somewhat unnecessary; it’s used early on in an introductory way, and then following that, it feels largely unimportant. After this, the most consistent minigame was one involving matching symbols to unlock doors or terminals. Lastly there are a whole series of other minigames that involves matching numbers or hitting a button at the right time, these were sparingly used and felt somewhat strange, along with some of the controls for opening levers and doors the control scheme makes more sense for a controller and felt antiquated for keyboard. 

Lastly, there is the Xenomorph itself, which presents a formidable foe with a distinct design in all aspects and is a terrifying presence once the game gets going. Its AI allows it to do some neat things and present a real challenge as it stalks you. It being such an undefeatable foe adds to this, and it did scare me a few times. 

As a big fan of the original movie this gave me everything I’d want out of a game in the universe mostly focusing on the horror and accurately creating the atmosphere. Separately, as a horror game, it provides a challenge and some genuinely scary moments, as well as a solid story and gameplay loop, while some aspects are unnecessarily involved, it remains an exceptional game.    

[h1]★★★★★[/h1]

217252111.review:
Brilliant game at the micro level - the spooks, the cat and mouse game with the Xenomorph, the classic survival horror item economy, everything is executed to near perfection. Simply unparalleled in translating that original film's core horror experience into interactive form.

But I have to admit, the almost decade-long consensus that the game is just too long for its own good rings true. It's paced incredibly well until Mission 10, about halfway through, but everything past that point is very much a repeat of your 10 or so hours so far. Suddenly, it becomes very apparent that all game you've been funneled from door to malfunctioning door, and made to go around tapping buttons and cranking power generators and its just all very banal in retrospect.

Still, for that very special distillation of true horror that the game does deliver so well all throughout the experience, I recommend it.

The sequel - if it addresses the complaints - stands to be one of the greatest games ever made, if they can manage to capture lightning in a bottle again like they did here with Isolation.

217006225.review:
Alien: Isolation is a really good, tense horror game set on Sevastapol, a derelict space station where Ripley has to navigate her way past aggressive humans, androids and the titular alien and find a way out alive. I have no connection to or experience with the Alien franchise, but this was still a highly enjoyable and honestly really scary experience. The sound design is incredible, the game looks great and the way the Alien stalks you, learns from what you do and will find ways to outsmart you is uniquely terrifying. 

Even so, as mentioned it's not the only danger; there are plenty of survivors willing to kill you and androids that are more than happy to take you apart, so stealth is often king. If that's not an option, you obtain several firearms throughout the game as well as gadgets you can craft with materials found on the station, such as EMP mines, pipe bombs and flashbangs, or fire-based weaponry that can even make the alien flee...for a little while at least.

If I had one complaint to give, it would be the length. As tense and interesting of an experience as Alien: Isolation is, it's also entirely too long for what it is. If you explore a little and get stuck a bit, it's almost a 20 hour game, maybe even longer. Personally (and that's my opinion), horror games shouldn't be too long because eventually, the horror wears off and all that remains is the gameplay. In this case, that means waiting long times in hiding spots for enemies or the Alien to pass or move away, constantly being very slow because crouching is a must most of the time or replaying entire sections because saving is only possible in predetermined spots and the Alien is really good at finding you. It's a scary game, but as impressive as it is, it does cross the line from terrifying to tedious about 10-11 hours in where you just wish the Alien would finally go terrorize someone else so you can move the story along a bit faster.

Still, I'm glad I saw it all the way through. Even as someone who doesn't care about the Alien franchise, this is a very good and atmospheric experience with many tense and terrifying moments. Play at night and with headphones on and it's guaranteed to make you anxious. A clear recommendation if you like horror games!

Instead of getting items that you can use, you get items that you have to find items for in order to use, and that means REexploring the areas you've already explored to find something that you should have already found and been able to carry, but you can't cuz you explored BEFORE the game wanted you to, so those 3 hours of moving very meticulously thru the immersive world were completely wasted cuz you didn't trigger a cutscene or go up to a terminal and activate it cuz you were exploring like any true gamer would.

I was never rewarded for exploring, and when the game gave me the elevator hacker after I explored the entire area, it told me I needed to find an item to FIX the elevator hack thing. 216529866.review:
This is such a fantastic game. Owned it on Xbox years ago and bought it again after ascending to the PC realm and finding it on sale for like $5. The game is 100% worth it's original price but if you're a bargain hunter then I would recommend waiting for it to go on sale again to save yourself some extra money that could be put toward purchasing the DLC. 

My only real gripe about the game is that the anti-aliasing is kind of awful. There is a mod that fixes the problem, however, it requires you to play with motion blur on. I can't stand motion blur in games so I'm stuck with the aliasing problems. If you've got a higher end Nvidia graphics card you can reduce the aliasing quite a bit by adjusting some settings in the Nvidia Control Panel. I can't speak for AMD as I haven't owned an AMD GPU.

Anyway, to reduce the aliasing and enhance the image quality go into the control panel, turn on "DSR - Factors" setting and select 1.78x & 2.25x. For those that may not know, this renders the game at a higher resolution then downscales it to your monitor's native resolution, but you have to select the resolution you want to render at in-game. Doing this drastically enhanced the image quality of the game and reduced some of the aliasing. 

To reduce it further, for your in-game anti-aliasing setting I would personally recommend using SMAA T2x if you've got the headroom. I'm currently on a 4070 Super and it uses between 75%-96% of my GPU with maximum Ultra settings and customized Control Panel settings. If you want to use less GPU power then SMAA T1x is still pretty good. Here are what each in-game anti-aliasing setting does:

--> FXAA: sharp, little blur, good default
--> SMAA T1x: better edges, slightly cleaner diagonal geometry, minimal blur
--> SMAA T2x: smoother camera motion, slight ghosting, small improvement at 4K

216505728.review:
The Xenomorph is hunting you.

It all starts innocuously enough -  the soft hum of machines, the squeaky scuffing of shoes against laminate flooring, the rhythmic beeping of tracking devices -  then an errant sound of metal scraping, of something tumbling in the ventilation shafts. [i]It's Here![/i] A scramble for cover, for anything to hide behind because you know what's coming. You desperately hope the sound of slamming the locker door shut as you hide in it isn't too loud. Then you hear it drop through the vents and hit the floor. You hear it [i]hissing.[/i] 

You don't even chance a look at your tracking device because any sound you make could be your last. This is Alien: Isolation. Fear, dread, and anticipation, while the relentless xenomorph chases you through starships and space stations. Can you survive? 

A high water mark for survival horror games, Alien: Isolation is scarily good fun.

216367871.review:
I’m not particularly one for the horror genre. I tend to avoid scary media and rarely will you ever see me play a horror game. 

Alien: Isolation is the exception. 

Alien Isolation takes the greatest elements from the 1979 film and boils it down into approximately 18 hours’ worth of exciting story. 

Something I loved about the film was its unique characteristics: the unpolished futuristic retro sci-fi sets, the eerie main title score, specifically the iconic flute, the biomechanical art coined by H.R Giger, the sparing and intentional use of the Xenomorph.

Ridley Scott does a fantastic job of making the characters believable. Every day, blue collared folk, completing a mundane, long-haul gig, complaining about corporate negligence all the while aboard a deep space vessel.

All of these elements are present in Alien Isolation. 

The difference?

You’re playing instead of watching.

[h2] Story [/h2]
Without getting into spoiler territory, you play as Amanda Ripley, (the unheard-of daughter of Ellen Ripley, but recognized officially as canon!) set 15 years after the events of the film. Amanda is on her journey to Sevastopol Station, investigating her mother’s disappearance.

The story is fantastic. You won’t be disappointed. Some reviews may argue that the game is not long enough to warrant the price that comes along with it. I would argue that the game still offers a complete package despite its shorter runtime. It’s no mystery that there were some cuts during development that would have given the game more life. Despite the cuts I do believe it’s worth the value. Keep it short and sweet.

[h2] Atmosphere [/h2]
Sevastopol Station is as [i] Alien [/i] as it gets. You walk through a permanent, large scale space station, scheduled for decommission, layered with the residue of daily life. The atmosphere in this game alone could warrant its own review. 

Sevastopol groans and shifts around you, like a submarine under pressure. Amplifying the tension in every corridor.

Vent propellors silently hum while inspection lamps flicker with each spin of the blades.

Discarded hallways filled with debris, now menaced by the threat that looms.

The motion tracker’s green screen pulses and beeps with every nearby movement, relaying the approaching threat to the cadence of your escalating heartbeat.

Rewire consoles chirp a playful 8-bit melody whilst the shadowy Seegson Corporation logo casts an unsettling presence.

Desks littered with various magazines and pamphlets, ashtrays left unkept, posters promoting Seegson’s ‘Working Joes’ line the station with a conditional benevolence.

And of course you can’t go without some fan service drinking birds!

[h2] Characters [/h2]
The characters you interact with along the way are grounded in their beliefs, motivations, and attitudes and feel appropriate to the situation. The voice work is great too. I’d recommend taking the time to listen to the audio tapes and read the logs. I think they do a great job of illustrating the overall sentiment on the station before and during the current predicament. Instead of making players sit through dialogue they make it optional. Optional world building FTW.

[h2] Replay Value [/h2]
I’ve returned to the game two or three times since purchase in 2017 and found it’s quite replayable. The graphics still hold up, the unpredictable AI encounters can diversify each area, and upping the difficulty can really change your playstyle. 

[h2] Alien's AI [/h2]
Here’s where I feel the game begins to truly shine – the Alien’s AI.

The Alien’s AI is unlike any I’ve experienced. The creature adapts in ways that feel intelligent rather than scripted. The creature learns your playstyle. Overused tactics become obsolete, especially if you play on higher difficulties. The game challenges players to rethink their approach which makes the length of the game more reconciling due to the evolving AI. 

There’s much more insight into the mechanics of the AI online, but if it’s your first playthrough I’d seriously recommend going in without the cheat sheet at your disposal. 

[h2] Sound Design [/h2]
The sound design faithfully mirrors the film. It’s a subtle nod that showcases the developers’ dedication to capturing the authentic Alien experience. The rough mechanical sounds of doors opening, to vents screeching as they spiral open or shut, the threating thuds from overhead, the announcements over the intercom, the foreboding alarm system. It was one of the first observations I made during my initial playthrough and one of my favorites.

[h2] Performance [/h2]
As for performance, the game runs beautifully. It’s an older game so most rigs can handle this game with ease. I have a modest rig with everything maxed and I’m running without any issues whatsoever.

[h2] Glitches/Bugs [/h2]
I’ve encountered very minimal bugs and glitches. Nothing severe or game breaking. I’ve had a few instances of floating objects – but they disappear before you realize it. I’ve run into a bug where Ripley will walk slower than usual, but upon a simple google search it’s due to having high FPS. It was rare and infrequent, so it never truly bothered me much. 

[h2] Overall [/h2]
I decided to return to the game after recent resurgence in Alien hype (i.e., Alien Romulus) and was not disappointed. I'll admit some bias, however; Alien Isolation stands confidently on its own as a unique survival horror game unlike anything I have experienced. Alien fans will enjoy themselves alongside newcomers who can find a self-contained gem that may coerce you to check out the original film. 

PS: If it provides any intrigue, know that Creative Assembly confirmed they're in early development for a sequel!!

215706774.review:
I went into Alien Isolation with the intention of getting completion, in my first playthrough I played in hard difficulty (as recommended by the game itself) with the intention of just completing the story and doing whichever side quest i could
For a 2014 game it still looks great, the atmosphere is immaculate, from the level design to the audio design. if I had to complain about one thing it would probably be that the Human and Android models felt a little outdated and low quality in comparison to the rest of the game.

The first hour of the game go as expected, just walking around familiarizing yourself with the ship and learning some of the protagonist's motivations, the story in my opinion is not that strong and sometimes it can be a little bit confusing figuring out what exactly you need to do but you stop caring about that right at the moment you get introduced to the Alien for the first time, you quickly realize you're playing a game of cat and mouse and you're not the cat, the Alien has one of the best artificial intelligences I've seen in a game, the whole time it feels like is learning how you play, where you hide, how you try to distract it and actively countering you, is genuinely some of the most on edge I ever felt playing a game, you cant trick the alien or learn his behavior, you never stop feeling like you're being hunted.

TLDR: The game looks great, feels great, the Alien feels like an actual threat that you can't trick or kill but the mission objectives are not that clear and sometimes it feels like the story goes on for too long.

Would I recommend the game? If you like the genre or you want to feel stressed for like 25 hours then yes, absolutely.
