215329968.review:
The scarriest part of this game was gathering the courage to start playing it. Then the scariest was the beginning, way before you encounter any enemies. It's just you and the vast empty station with abandoned rooms and hallways. It really conveyed the feeling of being alone in this huge station. Then you're presented with a crowd: survivors, thugs, synthetics, suddenly it's a circus and the spooky atmosphere is gone. Then the next dose of scare is when you finally meet the creature, but it dries out very qucikly. 30 minutes of sneaking around the Alien and I feel about as relaxed as walking in a park during a sunny day. I kid you not, this is the least scary horror game I've ever played. The Alien is insanely easy to avoid (apart one mission where it's constantly in your ass). And the thing is: if you get detected and you don't have a weapon to scare it away, it's a 99.9% guaranteed death (might as well reload at this point), and it you have a weapon then you'll 100% survive with no danger whatsoever. It would have been much better if you could't force the Alien to retreat but only stun it momentarily (with a cooldown before it could be stunned again) and then you'd have to quickly get away and hide.

Now about the "revolutionary" AI, it's 2 things:
1 - It's tethered to you, meaning it will always lurk in your general area. Horror games have been doing this for long. Except in other games the enemy just teleports (e.g SOMA), here the Alien actually traverses out of bounds above the map using the ceiling "vents".
2 - It actively searches the area using it's sight and hearing. Again, this has been in videogames since forever.

There are a few bugs, like enemies freezing, and a few forced walking sections towards the end that are a total snooze.

I also encountered some weird looping behavior from the Alien: it comes out of a vent, scans 2 corridors for a minute, enters a vent and immediately leaves another vent 10 meters away, then repeat the same pattern. It repeated this 5-6 times before I could progress as it blocked my path.
The other one, I load the game, go into a room and hide in a closet, Alien comes, checks the closed and I die. No matter how many times I reloaded, it repeated the exact same behavior. And the funny thing is, unlike in other games such as Outlast where enemies randomly check hiding spots, here the Alien will only ever check the a spot if you're hiding there, otherwise it won't even bother.

Despite all this, it's a very enjoyable game, just not scary, but it's very satisfying to outplay the silly Alien-chan.

215098965.review:
Ever since my resurgence in the interest of the AVP universe, I have slowly been getting back into Alien and Predator media. It started with watching the original trilogy, seeing Romulus day one in theaters, and now I'm here. When I was younger the first Alien movie captivated me more than any other horror franchise. The perfect lifeform just killing people in space cause it wants to. Never the less this game captures my first watch of Alien and even makes me the person getting hunted.

The atmosphere is unmatched by any other horror game I've played, the sound being ripped straight from the movies, the music when you are actively being hunted by the alien, and especially when the tense moments where you motion tracker starts freaking out. I'd say there's never a single moment where you are NOT safe from the alien, Seegson synthetics, or the anarchistic humans.

The gameplay will consist of you hitting that crouch button a lot. This game heavily encourages stealthing through encounters and using firearms as a last resort. You will have the most success with firearms against humans, slightly less success against the synths but all the same results, and NO success against the alien. As you progress through the game your arsenal of tools and weapons will grow. Most of the tools will be found with blueprint scattered around the station while weapons are earned through progression of the story. All enemies can be fooled to your content and how much distractions you have left in your inventory. All except one. But don't worry about that right now. The game does contain an open world of sorts. It's more akin to hub areas that you travel to. You can always revisit older areas and are encouraged to as there are resources locked behind tools given later in the game. 

Ellen's daughter, Amanda, being used as the protagonist was a great idea from the devs. This game takes place after the first movie, when The Nostromo was found by another ship, and brought to Sevastopol station where.... well you know how that probably went. Using Ellen's daughter for me had two motive. One, to draw in older fans of the franchise as a sort of borderline nostalgia bait. Two, as a way to continue Ellen's story through her daughter. Ellen died at the end of Alien 3 and so if there is ever an interest to continue the Ripley story (yes I know there's a sequel in early development but who knows if we're playing as Amanda again) then Amanda is the perfect candidate and I wouldn't have any other person be it. 

I feel like I must touch on the later difficulty with the Alien. As you progress through the game, the alien learns your tactics, whether you like to hide in lockers or under desks, it will check there as opposed to you first non scripted encounter with it. Think you might wanna throw a flare? Sorry kid it knows that it's a fake. How about the flamethrower? It will become more fearless to the flames as you use it. Some may not like this but this is what we call GOOD GAME DESIGN. It forces you to switch your strategies to escape deadly encounters. This ain't your momma's horror game we watch people get slaughtered by various horrors that we are barely defensible against. Back onto the alien's behavior. This hidden form of raising difficulty is what separates its competitors, creating an engaging game of cat and mouse, the likes of which we have yet to see from any other horror game. 

For those curious as to how the alien can figure out where you are, where a quick lesson. The alien operates off two different AIs. The director and the navigator AI. The director directs the navigator to your supposed position based off sound and eventually hiding habits. Basically the alien is getting back seated. You can throw off the alien but old habits die young and so you'll have to find new ways to ♥♥♥♥ with the director AI.

 This game stand tall with all the other singe player horror franchises such as Outlast, Dead Space, Silent Hill, Resident Evil, etc. and will always remain so. There has never been a better time to play this game too as it frequently is on sale so now I just gave you an excuse to buy this. Go open up that wallet. You won't regret it with this one. Do it. Buy it. You know you want to.

214721301.review:
Before saying anything negative, I'll just put it out there that this is an excellent game. Well made, gorgeous especially for its age, and tight gameplay. It felt really in line with the proper Alien vibe (aka Alien & Aliens, the good ones), and clearly had a ton of love put into it. If you enjoy survival games, sci-fi, and being challenged, you'll dig this game. 

But there's definitely some negatives worth knowing well ahead. This is a lot of game. A LOT. Not necessarily a LONG game, but it takes quite a long time to progress in the game. Some scenes move quickly, but many others can take you more than an hour just to figure out how to get across a tricky section of map without dying. Which you'll likely do ad infinitum. At times the gameplay feels more like a puzzle game akin to Abe's Odyssey than survival just in how many times you'll have to die just to figure out the trick to a certain spot, only with a chaotic alien showing up randomly to ruin your day. This can lead to habitually spam saving just to save yourself having to needlessly redo things, as it can sometimes feel quite out of your control when you'll die or not. There were moments of monumental slog that I really had to work through, but I beat the game so it couldn't have been that bad. 

All that said, I still find this an excellent game. If anything it's only downside is being TOO near realistic (apart from the alien vanishing far away and then reappearing right above you...♥♥♥♥ that) and too unforgiving. Expect to put in 50% more effort and sheer time than you might have expected, but luckily at least 75% of it will be really enjoyable.

214531944.review:
[h1]“In Space No One Can Hear You Scream”[/h1]
Based on the Alien film series, the game is set 15 years after the original 1979 film, and follows the engineer Amanda Ripley. Amanda investigates the disappearance of her mother, Ellen Ripley, aboard the space station Sevastopol, which is in disarray due to years of corporate negligence and the threat of a rampant alien creature. The game emphasizes stealth and survival horror gameplay, requiring the player to avoid, outsmart, and fight various enemies.

[url=https://steamcommunity.com/groups/ATReviews]Follow My Review Group Here![/url]

💾Graphics - Alien Isolation still holds strong with its visuals. The lighting is creepy, well placed in many areas of the game, and looks really smooth. I feel like the lighting plays a big role here with the level design and atmosphere. In my opinion, it’s some of the best in gaming when it comes to horror ambiance. I do like the retro style AI that actually fits with the Alien franchise. The team did a fantastic job with making the items look just like from the movies. This adds to the immersion a lot, especially if you are an Alien fan. The only thing I don’t like is the jagged edges on the objects. The game could have had an update with the anti aliasing since it’s kinda rubbish. To some it can be distracting, but not a game breaker. Overall the game looks incredible and totally fits the Alien aesthetic from the older movies.

🎮Gameplay - Alien Isolation is pretty okay. It’s nothing deep, but it gets the job done. The real shine is the encounters and level design for me. The encounters require a good bit of strategy when it comes to hiding, flanking around, using a specific weapon and so on. This can make the game feel tense and quite rewarding actually. Also the level design is truly incredible. I love the many different areas to explore and how this game truly feels like an authentic experience that not many games really capture that well. I also love the motion sensor! It tells you where to go and tells you where everyone is, just like the movies. You can craft items and unlock different areas with puzzles and keys. The puzzles in this game are really easy and can get repetitive pretty quickly. Also the human enemies can be pretty annoying to fight. My biggest complaint is the infinite ammo these people have which can make encounters frustrating and a pain in the a*s at times. The best part in the Alien. The way it hunts and always puts you on edge was designed perfectly, especially with the amazing level design. Yes there are a few flaws, but the overall experience with the gameplay was really good.

📜Story - The story is actually pretty cool since it’s kinda like a sequel to the Alien movie. The year is 2137 and you play as Amanda Ripley, daughter to Ellen Ripley who went missing 15 years ago. The flight recorder from your mothers ship the Nostromo has been found and is currently being held at Sevastopol. You are being sent on the salvage mission with a small team aboard the Torrens to finally get some answers. Once you arrive you notice the ship seems damaged and is not communicating. Since this ship is not communicating, your team decides to investigate, which leads to a domino effect of terrifying events for you and your crew.  This story was so fun to go through. You will experience many crazy and scary moments throughout the story which makes the story pretty memorable.

🕛Playtime - The game can take around 20+ hours to beat. There’s not much replayability besides going for achievements. You do have many DLC’s you can try that will bring the playtime higher, and plus the DLC’s are pretty good as well.

🎧Sound - The sound design is truly special! Many of the sounds sound close to the movies, for example the siren alarms or the computer sounds. Every sound in this game just fits so perfectly. The sound ambiance is scary, the robots are creepy, and the alien sounds are absolutely nightmare fuel! The alien sounds and it wandering around you is like PTSD levels scary. Hearing the Alien in the vents never gets easy for me. Sometimes I just sit in a corner while the Alien is moving around because I get so scared lol. The music too is so reminiscent of the original movies. The sounds are just pure perfection in every aspect.

❗Bugs❗- The biggest bug I have experienced is high framerate issues. It can break the game for me. For example, doors won’t open, and sound queues are delayed because of the high FPS. I highly recommend you place your FPS to 100 or lower. This completely fixed the issues for me.

🔅Performance - This game runs so incredibly well! Definitely a well optimized game. I had no crashes or stutters. The game ran like a charm for me. I did lock my fps to 80 so I didn’t get any engine issues. Besides that you will have no issues running this game.

✔️Pro’s✔️
[list]
	[*]Great optimization
	[*]Superb sound design
	[*]Amazing level design
	[*]Good stealth mechanics
	[*]Incredible visuals & lighting
	[*]Enemy encounters require strategy
	[*]Alien AI is intelligence & unpredictable
[/list]

❌Con’s❌
[list]
	[*]Some bad engine bugs with high FPS
	[*]Human encounters have infinite ammo
	[*]Anti-aliasing is pretty rough (jagged edges are common)
[/list]
        
🏆Rating - 9/10
I’d wait for this game to be like $20 dollars, especially if you plan on getting all the DLC’s.

🔗Conclusion - Alien Isolation captures the most authentic experience if you are a fan of the Alien movies. The game is absolutely terrifying and challenging with an incredible atmosphere. The AI is challenging, and the Alien Stalker enemy can make the game feel like a true nightmare. I can say many great things about Alien Isolation. Yes it does have some flaws, but if you love the Alien movies or just want to experience an incredible horror game, then you must play this game!

https://store.steampowered.com/app/214490/Alien_Isolation/

214082891.review:
The atmosphere and sound design are excellent but it doesn't do something interesting story wise. A female protagonist going against an alien and evil corporation? we’ve seen that already. Rather than expanding the world or taking risks it just plays it safe and recycles familiar ideas. Working Joes is probably the most interesting addition.
For some reason there are aliens around and the station is still full of armed human gangs roaming the halls like the alien and androids are no big deal which feels completely out of place.
Gameplay-wise it’s really repetitive. The puzzles are trivial and reused constantly, turning what should be tension into routine. There are long tedious on-rails walking sections where you’re forced to walk at a snail’s pace.
There are way too many goofy weapons most of which I didn't even bothered to use. Being able to carry that so many weapons makes zero sense for an immersive horror game.
Overall, the game just drags because you feel the game repeating itself. I just wanted to wander the station, feel the dread and be isolated. And not have characters talking/explaining things and dumping information over comms afraid of letting the players figure out things by themselves.
Also Ripley getting herself out of the nest is unbelieavable.

213897698.review:
an Amazing stealth horror that overstays its welcome.

Halfway through my playthrough even when having fun, I was like “when does this game end bro”, it’s certainly too long for what it is and that’s my biggest grip, the pacing is very inconsistent and some objectives feels unnecessary for the plot and are just there to add more playtime. I feel like if it was about 8 - 10 hours long It would’ve been alot better and much more replayable.

Aside from its length, i like this game. The story is good, nothing crazy but it’s interesting enough to go through it, Filed with cool characters that are fleshed out just enough to serve a purpose.

The gameplay is okay, the combat is fine but they’re both not top tier, so don’t come in expecting something like resident evil 4, it plays a lot more like outlast but far deeper and more engaging, rather than just running away, expect to shoot a few people (or aliens and killing machines) along the way, you’re not completely hopeless but you would feel like it throughout the entire game.

The puzzles are another weak point, they’re far too simple and can be solved by putting the least amount of effort into it. Did they honestly think pulling down levers and some QTE’s was good enough to be considered a puzzle? It’s not an issue but it would be nice to do something that requires me to use my high iq Harvard brain (lie) sometimes. 

By far the biggest standout is the stalker enemy, the xenomorph did not disappoint in any way, I anticipated him to be the next Mr X and he absolutely was. The game shines whenever he’s around, the constant feeling that he’s behind you and the way he hears and punishes any noise you make truly makes him one of the best stalkers ever, he will not let you go, he’ll search the whole ship and every hiding spot just to kill you.

It’s not just the stalker that’s unique, the joes serve their purpose to the fullest, making you feel hopeless knowing that you can’t kill them unless you empty your revolver. The game encourages you to run away and it executed this tactic in a way that feels entertaining rather than making me feel fatigued.

Overall alien isolation is a good game that would’ve been much better if it wasn’t as long as it is, all of its other flaws would be negligible if it wasn’t for that but the cheer scope of it is what makes these issues standout. 7.25/10, get it on a discount.

213620254.review:
If you’re looking for a game that actually respects the original 1979 film, this is it. It’s easily the most stressful 20 hours I’ve ever spent in a video game, but in a way that makes you want to keep coming back for more.

The [b]atmosphere[/b] is the real star here. Sevastopol Station feels alive—or rather, dying—with its clunky, lo-fi tech and flickering lights. The sound design is terrifying; hearing the heavy thud of the Xenomorph in the vents above you while you’re staring at that green CRT motion tracker is a level of tension I haven’t found anywhere else.

What makes it work is that the [b]AI[/b] doesn't follow a set path. It’s genuinely hunting you. You can't just memorize its route; you have to adapt. There were times I spent ten minutes under a desk just waiting for the right moment to move, and when you finally reach one of those emergency save terminals, the relief is physical.

It does drag a little bit in the final third, and the "Working Joe" androids can be more annoying than scary sometimes, but the overall experience is a masterpiece of survival horror.

[b]Highly recommended[/b] if you have the patience for a slow-burn and don't mind a high heart rate.

213611864.review:
The atmosphere is a feast for eyes and ears. Retrofuturistic interiors blended with tactile sound design. Optimistic space age fashion replaced with efficiency of brutalism. Lo-fi aestethics of bulky analog computers, hostile user interfaces, noisy drives and clanky keyboards. All these stimuli remind you of an age long gone, when the computers were not just blended in the background and screamed "hey I'm doing something!" whenever they worked.

The gameplay is advanced hide-and-seek. What if there were multiple types of seekers, how would they interact with each other? Can you use one against another or would they cover each other's weaknesses? What if both the seeker and the hider gradually "learn" from each other and attempt to deceive one another in different ways? etc.

One of the seeker types and the co-star of the show are the androids. Their design definitely lies in the uncanny valley. "The Working Joes" don't try hard enough to look and behave like a human. They are not there to look appealing or friendly while working, only to perform the menial tasks or whatever they are commanded to do.
They are just computers attached to a skeletal chassis with a rudimentary mask on. They look so lifeless in fact that the Alien simply does not register them as a threat and completely ignores them. I really enjoyed this contrast between Seegson and WY synths.

And the star of the show, the ultimate stalker, the Xenomorph. Its non-deterministic beheaviour and level design make up for great stories and unforgettable moments. While it carefully searches around you in the early game, later it frantically scours the place for you, performs fake-outs to lure you out, stays still to disappear in your motion tracker and gets frustrated when distracted by your gadgets. In the end you really respect it for what it is. 

The plot is very much reminiscent of the original movie. The age old tale of corporate greed and expendibality of human lives for the sake of it.
As much as I find this topic important, it felt a bit dry and like a retelling of the original story. With a different place, company and characters but it all comes back to what happened on Nostromo and how WY was and is responsible for everything. The logs spread around Sevastopol reveal how the company executives operate and view the world and how little they think of others whereas common people simply try to survive in the apocalyptic aftermath of their decisions.

What the game leaves behind for me apart from the pretty screenshots are the sounds that I always kept track of. Like Xeno's stomps, hisses or the dreading sound of it falling onto the ground from a vent. They are etched into my mind at this point and I hope the horror stays with me forever.

213551364.review:
This is my favourite horror game of all time, out of all the Frictional, Silent Hill, Resident Evil, all of them, this game is the best. 

It nails the disempowerment, the Alien AI makes you feel unsafe wherever you are, and it is never predictible. 

A masterpiece of the genre.

213539830.review:
Do not Eat or drink 48 hours before playing this game 
You will be scared ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ☠︎ =

Although the story is pretty basic, Alien Isolation is extremely immersive. 
Pure tension, incredible atmosphere, game still holds up 

The Alien, although cheap at times, is adaptive to your behaviour. Hide too often, it searches more places. Overuse the flamethrower & it becomes resistant. Same with explosives & distractions, the AI will learn / adapt,

Some areas drag others are amazing, humans shooting at you is a little dumb, but they've been surviving in a state of anarchy before Ripley arrives. So their defensiveness & desperation is plausible. I mentioned the story being basic, as its mostly repairing and rerouting different systems across the ship. But it doesnt stop the game from having some great kino moments, both within the story & the chaotic ones you will make. Androids can be tedious, but with the right gear + trial & error, they make the learning curve feel rewarding.

Audio is brilliant, as are the environments, very faithful to the franchise. You dont need to be familiar, or even like the Alien series to appreciate this game. I would definitely recommend this, if you like Sci Fi or want a challenge. Especially if you love a level of horror which might give you nightmares, ptsd, heart attacks & loss of social skills.

Just as good to replay today & puts many AAA titles to shame - 10/10

213104081.review:
[h1] TLDR: 7/10 Great Atmosphere With a Faithful Aesthetic That Fits Snugly Into the Movie Canon. This Is Literally an Alien Movie That You Control the Pacing Of. Or Rather This Is an Alien Movie That the Alien Controls the Pacing Of [/h1]

[h3] Actual Review (Gameplay Spoilers Ahead) [/h3]
So much detail packed into the world of this game. The Alien franchise fits perfectly with the survival horror genre, but the real treat is this game's incredible pacing; both micro and macro. There is breathing room but never for too long. There is the tradeoff between risking death scavenging or pressing forward undersupplied. There is a healthy selection of enemies and gear. And there's also playing the waiting game because the alien won't stop camping directly on top of the desk you're under.

Yeah so this game has a few issues, and they mostly come from sections where the Xeno is the main threat. You most likely will be stuck early on at some point while trying to understand how the AI works. The issue is this completely destroys the scare factor of the game and you will instead start treating Xeno encounters like a puzzle to be optimized instead of a immersive horror scenario. This is the natural fate of all horror games, but it starts happening from the very first encounter and made me just throw trying to play the game like an interactive movie out the window.

The second playthrough for 100% was actually kinda more fun than the first. Knowing how the enemies work and not having to stumble around new rooms under pressure was far less annoying. Plus mopping up all the extra logs and missed lore stuff was great because of all the tie-ins with the movies. No difficult achievements, even the challenge and hard mode ones. The AI in this game might be impressive under the hood, but it is consistent in the ways you can manipulate it. It's more about picking the right positioning before you make a move.

Worth a playthrough or two even if you know nothing about the franchise. You should probably at least watch the original movie first though, it makes the game so much better. 7/10

213010792.review:
No choice but to hide and pray.
From the moment I’m thrown into this brutal, mechanical nightmare, the oppressive atmosphere tightens around me like a vice. Every distant clang prickles my nerves, every shifting shadow sends my heart sinking, and every single beep of the motion tracker is a reminder of the unstoppable creature stalking me, lurking, calculating, insatiable. the perfect organism that eventually I'll have to face. My only lifelines are a handful of improvised tools and the stubborn will to push forward, to outthink a being built to hunt, and to bring this waking nightmare to an end.

Alien: Isolation surrounds you with a soundscape crafted with unnerving precision and an environment dripping with detail, corridors and consoles that look and feel ripped straight from a piece of cold, intricate mechanical art. Add to that a relentless enemy waiting for the slightest mistake, and what emerges is a game that stands as both a love letter to the Alien franchise and a masterclass in what it truly means to experience survival horror.

Would isolate the alien anytime o(*￣▽￣*)o

213005185.review:
I was tempted to leave a negative review, but I won't - partly because the game is very unique and partly because I believe I unintentionally made the first experience worse by playing on Hard. The Alien’s AI - whilst definitely impressive for its time - on Hard becomes often tedious, overly persistent, and outright exhausting before you get the flamethrower. It made me quit multiple times, and I was genuinely close to uninstalling. You spend a lot of time waiting for Alien to leave, dying in stupid ways and hoping that this time you'll actually be able to progress the story.

Once I got the flamethrower, the game improved drastically. You regain agency, stealth becomes an actual system rather than a punishment, and the pacing opens up. From that point on, the game is brilliant: atmosphere, lighting, locations, gameplay, story and overall design are all outstanding. I'm very happy I finally played it considering I've wanted to since I was 15. 

I do recommend the game - but do not start it on Hard unless you're ready for frustration. I haven't played on Normal but from what I've read, it fixes most of my issues.

As someone who doesn’t really play stealth games, doesn’t like first-person games, and isn’t big on the Alien franchise (aside from Prometheus), I enjoyed this game quite a bit.

The devs did a great job translating the panic of the first film into a game. The Medical Lab chapters is terrifying and probably a moment I’ll never forget playing.

It does overstay its welcome and becomes a bit too repetitive by the end. Part of that repetition come from the story, where they use the “Yes, but/ No, and” method of storytelling, but usually the answers to those setups feel the same every time.

Great game though and one of my favorites that I’ve played this year. It also plays great on the Steam Deck. I was locked at 90fps with barely any dips and it looked beautiful. I can’t remember the last time I played a AAA title that was this smooth on the Deck.

212734756.review:
Alien: Isolation is a game that I've been playing for many years and I've finished it many times on different platforms.
The game still manages to scare me, even as an experienced player - the Xenomorphs AI truly is the best I've seen in any game.

For many reasons this game is my favorite game of all time, but one of the key reasons as to why is simple - the atmosphere, environment & Alien.

The atmosphere, environment and ambience are perfect. And with the constant threat of the Alien or Android make this (in my opinion) one of the scariest horror games of all time. The androids are easy to counter and avoid - while the Alien on the other hand is harder. The game succeeds at reminding you that you are not in control, and it's especially good at reminding you with the AI that the Alien has. I've never seen a smarter enemy in a video game before than in Alien: Isolation. And even after playing this game for years the Alien is still unpredictable and it still scares me whenever I get chased by it, or when it does something that I didn't expect.

This game is a mastercraft of horror that I hope the sequel can replicate or even do better.

If you still haven't, get this game - because it is simply fantastic. And remember - don't run.

Lets not beat about the bush, the game is bad. Not just a bad stealth game, but a bad game in general, because apart from stealth there is hardly anything in it left.
First there is one thing that that game does exceptionally well – it is aesthetics. Some people would add “and atmosphere” but they would be lying. Watching movies I have never ever thought “by the void this is so tedious and annoying” - and in the game once alien is introduced any horror it induced first few times I saw it was quickly replaced with above mentioned feeling. The implementation of Alien AI is just infuriatingly bad. I do not understand about people gushing about this. I always knows your relative position and always skulks around until some counter or another ticks off and SUDDENY it finds you, irrelevant to any of your actions. Its like some dev implements that at random intervals 10-60 seconds game rolls random number 1-20 and if it is 1 it gives U DIED screen. Unpredictable? Yes. Good game design? You decide.
Not to mention the bouts of stupidity of astronomical proportions. One time I have activated a computer which started to play a video and I hear that alien just got out of some vent right next to me. Literally few pixels away. And was huffing and puffing into my left ear through all the video until it finished. Then I turned left, we looked into each other, he turned 90 degrees and walked out of the room. PERFECT ORGANISM MY FOOT.
Or how about I am in closed room I walked to check for crafting materials, and I hear alien coming , I hide in to the closet, alien skulks around the closed for like half a minute walks out, I am ready to leave the closet – and he comes back immediately. on a 5 star scale i think i would give 6 stars...

IMHO a masterpiece and as a fan of alien franchise and games with a good SP campaign i recommend to anybody

* The story ... absolutely nailed it - its like being in the movie ... they could literally make this as a Alien movie - long enough to have ton of fun but short enough to not suffer exhaustion
** The atmosphere ... absolutely tense and visually nailed it ... Alien and Aliens movie vibe so well reproduced (i would add alien romulus but i guess its the other way around here)
*** The graphics ... well released in 2014 - and you can see it in some aspects - the visuals are still well enough so it aged well and the surrounding is reproduced with attention to detail (actually much better than Alien Rogue Incursion pt.1 which seemed dull - this one has the look and grit)
**** The soundtrack ... with all sounds exactly like in the movies and a rich and realistic layer and layer of sound effects feeding the senses 
***** The gameplay ... holy moly ... the fact that you rarely encounter the alien but its constantly somewhere close is creating this constant fear factor - just like in the movies - 
****** The immersion ... so all the above points and the balance of them all add to the fact that this game stands out in how immersive it is. a sp campaign for me should look like this - being an interactive version of a top notch blockbuster that makes u dive into the story/universe and by that define its entertainment value

At the same time a sad reminder - todays games are often graphically better but lack the immersion. 
i hope that game devs find their way back to creating legendary games like this

211498521.review:
(human 1) OMG, this space station is about to blow!
(human 2) And there's something running around killing us!
(human 1) Are you thinking what I'm thinking?
(human 2) That we should turn on each other and start gang wars?
(human 1) OMG, Jinx! BESTIES!
*Both start shooting each  other in a pointless gun fight and die*

This is the mind-boggling stupidity you'll be dealing with in this game.

Is this an aliens game? Yes.
Does it bank on the cat-n-mouse horror aspect instead of guns-a-blazin? Yes.

Sounds like we have a winner on our hands. Until you get into the thick of it all. The humans are fighting each other. An alien shows up forcing you to use the lame stealth mechanics. You have this massive space station you need to wander around, often wondering "where do I go? what do I do?"

Being the cat-n-mouse horror it is, they added puzzle elements, like make you sweat...

"you need to reroute power from certain control panels to make this door work."
Ok, fine.
"Oh, and the alien is here"
Oh.. that's not good.
"Yeah. It's faster than you, and kills you on-sight."
Oh noes!
"Oh, and those guns of yours... Yeah, those will only make it angry."
Wait, Space Marines were shooting aliens right n left with shotguns and machine guns
"Yeah... this isn't that kind of game."

O.o

Well good lord, the franchise can't even keep any continuity between how powerful these aliens are and how powerful weapons are. It all feels like "plot contrivance / plot convenience" that's forced upon you to limit player agency to make the ham-fisted plot work.

From the very start you find out that humans are fighting each other (and you roll your eyes, b/c that's the least helpful thing that could happen in the middle of a crisis), you eventually find some humans trying to kill you with guns. Ok, I've got a crowbar, they've got a gun. I'll just hide here, whack-a-mole this guy, and take his... Wait, why can't I take his gun?

Oh, you have to go FIND the one specific gun that has the cut scene attached to it that makes picking up that one specific gun SPECIAL.

*rolls eyes*

I'm really, REALLY having a hard time getting into this game and sticking with it, because it's just so insulting in how it treats you. It's a so-so cat-n-mouse game. It has a so-so plot. It has contrived drama to artificially ramp up the stakes.

When I started writing this I was of the "wish I could give it a 'meh' but I'll give it a thumbs up." But, now that I'm thinking back on it all, I'm going to thumbs-down this. I just can't stand games that insult my intelligence these days.

211048527.review:
This is a must-play for fans of the Alien films and hardcore survival-horror enthusiasts. While the game performed poorly in both media reviews and sales, once you dig deeper, you’ll find it is an exceptionally well-crafted experience.

First of all, the game shows tremendous respect for the original film’s world-building. The tight, claustrophobic spaces, oppressive color palette, the protagonist’s limited capabilities, and the fact that the Xenomorph is completely unkillable—all of these perfectly recreate the sense of dread aboard a space station. Unlike many survival-horror games where abundant late-game resources lead to a drop in difficulty, Alien: Isolation keeps the tension high throughout. Even after acquiring the powerful flamethrower, the introduction of facehuggers and additional Xenomorph encounters ensures the experience remains consistently intense and nerve-wracking.

Of course, the protagonist’s weakness inevitably raises the difficulty. Combined with level design that is not always top-tier, players often die in ways that feel abrupt or unfair. This issue becomes increasingly frustrating in the later sections of the game, where the pacing—already quite slow—starts to feel suffocating. A tighter campaign with fewer repetitive segments would undoubtedly have made the experience even better.

Yet it is precisely this stubborn commitment to vision—this refusal to compromise—that gave birth to such a unique and unforgettable horror game. But that commitment comes at a high price. Through this game, I seem to see a dilemma inherent to the horror genre: the better its core horror element is crafted, the less commercially appealing the game becomes, and in the end, it can only become a form of self-entertainment for a small group of people.

[h3]8.5/10[/h3]

210746022.review:
Might be one of the greatest survival horror experience. I'm actually stunned by the quality of this game and it's part of the very few space horror game that stays true to the core of it.

I've done on hard and for someone who's used to playing games and horro games, it is the good difficulty. Well by the end of the game like, few chapter before the end, the Alien became so reckless I had to retry so much and the game wouldn't give me some fire to make him away (which is the easy way). Otherwise, I had big fun playing with it.

The whole atmosphere is great, you're in pretty quickly and the game doesn't wait to put you in bad situations. There isn't a single good situation moment. You're actually fight for your life through it all and you never feel safe. There's not a single safe space, saves are placed around the whole ship and you can actually be killed while saving. Hell, in any situations you can get killed, especially with the alien. That really a good point of the game, some might not like it but I tihnk it's great to always feel unsafe. 

Gameplay wise is really great too. You can so many options to overcome everything. Making noises, use a gun, distract, run or sneaking around, every type of ideas you can try might result or not on something. You got like 4 weapons, health item and many diff tools you can craft. And you will actually use every one of them. 

Story is what I can call space horror catastrophe with everything happening to Ripley. She can't get a single breath during 15/20 Hours of game (yes it is around how much I did to complete the game) During the whole game you try to escape from the station but things get complicated more and more and more but not in a repetitive way. Even if it feels like you never see the end, that the feeling the characters got too, it's so great. 

So yep, I love this game very much. I highly recommend it !

210256765.review:
Jedna z najlepszych adaptacji filmu w jakie dane jest nam zagrać. Alien: Isolation to horror, który fenomenalnie odzwierciedla klimat klasyka wyreżyserowanego przez Ridleya Scotta. Gra pozwala nam poczuć się jakbyśmy sami znaleźli się na stacji kosmicznej z morderczym ksenomorfem. Sevastopol po którym przyszło nam się poruszać wygląda jak żywcem wyciągnięty z ekranu. Fani oryginału odnajdą tu wiele smaczków i nawiązań do filmu. Graficznie na dzisiejsze standardy gra być może nie powala, ale twórcy zadbali o zaimplementowanie masy detali, aby odwzorować retro-futurystyczny klimat. Lokacje nie są zbyt różnorodne, ale tu znów niezbyt można było pozwolić sobie na kreatywność przy projektowaniu. Co natomiast stoi na najwyższym poziomie to udźwiękowienie. Odgłosy Obcego wybijającego symfonię w wentylacji zostają w głowie długo po zakończeniu sesji. Równie dobrze brzmią wszystkie inne dźwięki związane ze stacją i gadżetami. Podstawą gameplayu jest wykonywanie kolejnych zadań na stacji i jak najsprawniejsze unikanie konfrontacji z nieprzyjaciółmi. Ci dzielą się na trzy typy. Oczywiście głównym opresorem jest tytułowy ksenomorf. Ten zaprojektowany jest genialnie. Jego ruchy są nieprzewidywalne, więc przy każdym starciu na bieżąco musimy się adaptować i korzystać z wszelkich odblokowanych gadżetów. Nawet po wczytaniu save'a nasz gnębiciel potrafi pojawiać się w różnych miejscach i nigdy nie będzie nam dane przewidzieć co zrobi. Niestety sztuczna inteligencja ludzi i androidów znacznie odstaje od głównego antagonisty. O ile w przypadku androidów można przymknąć na to oko i nawet zrozumieć, że są to po prostu powolne roboty, które nie grzeszą inteligencją to starcia z ludźmi bywają okropne. Poruszają się oni głownie po z góry zaprojektowanych ścieżkach, ale zdarza się, że potrafią nas wypatrzeć i rozpocząć agresję bez żadnego powodu. Stoją wtedy bezmyślnie w miejscu i prują z broni palnej. Całe szczęście prowokuje to również Obcego, więc czasami służą mu jako przystawka. Co warto wspomnieć to gra fantastycznie wytwarza uczucie zaszczucia. Praktycznie nie ma miejsc, w których możemy czuć się w pełni bezpiecznie, bo Alien potrafi dostać się nawet do miejsc, gdzie rozstawione są miejsca do zapisu. Każda nieprzemyślanie rozpoczęta akcja jak wspomniane zapisywanie gry, otwieranie drzwi czy nawet czytanie dokumentów może zakończyć się zgonem. Niestety zadania z którymi przyjdzie nam się mierzyć są dość monotonne i brakuje mi tu bardziej rozwiniętych zagadek. Zadbano o pewną ilość minigierek przy otwieraniu i hakowaniu drzwi, ale większość misji polega na dostaniu się do konkretnego punktu i pociągnięcia za dźwignię. Uważam, że gra zyskałaby gdyby została nieco skrócona. Często zdarza się, że idziemy z punktu A do B, tylko po to aby zaraz wracać się po tej samej trasie. Oczywiście mierzymy się przy tym z Obcym, ale to wciąż żmudny backtracking. Same starcia rozwijają się z biegiem czasu. Na początku to typowa zabawa w chowanego, gdyż wykrycie przez ciemiężyciela zawsze kończy się zgonem. Z czasem odblokowujemy gadżety pomagające nam sprawniej wymijać oprawcę, a nawet chwilowo się go pozbyć. W tym miejscu polecam granie na najwyższym poziomie trudności, aby nie czuć się zbyt komfortowo z zasobami i podtrzymać uczucie zaszczucia. Niejednokrotnie napotkałem się na drobne błędy graficzne jak obiekty wiszące w powietrzu, ale to detal, który nie wpływa na ocenę. Mimo swoich bolączek, uważam, że jest to gra na tyle unikalna i potrafiąca przyspieszyć bicie serca, że należy jej się podbić punktację o przynajmniej jedno oczko.

This is less of a recommend and more of a "Incase you were curious"

A visually intriguing experience in the world of Alien, with a heavy amount of fan service and some new lore tastefully injected into the franchise. 

Gameplay starts out fairly interesting, but grows old quick due to a painfully long campaign, repetitive sequences, and literally 2 types of enemies, one of which is completely invincible and you spend 90% of the game hiding from, of course.

The story was alright, and I do like that some of the DLC recreates moments from the original Alien, but it wasn't groundbreaking, nor was it a particular highlight of the Alien series.

208776259.review:
Okay, just got past the awful intro sequence with the insufferable Axel to find the worst horror game I've ever played. Every system is clunky and obtuse, the voice acting is awful, cutscenes look terrible, far too many systems to keep track of, and the game is far too linear of an experience. If you want to have to reload saves a dozen times until you sneak in the one particular line that the game expects you to, this will be incredibly fun. I thought I was supposed to be hiding from an alien, but so far the gameplay loop consists of "npcs with guns spawn after you do thing, you're supposed to preternaturally know where to go, and if you don't you die". And with no hint system and poor map design, the player is left in the dark grasping at where to go.

I think I was spoiled by Amnesia: The Bunker. I thought this would be in the same vein with the stalker type enemy, but whereas The Bunker is open-ended, with clear goals and a map that can be memorized, Alien: Isolation is somehow too simple and too complex, too hand-holdy and too cryptic. GTA style missions which can only be completed one way do NOT belong in a horror game, period. Also, I don't play horror games to be shot at by humans and get lost in dark, generic scifi hallways which you can't tell apart. This map has no substance, and the player has too many restrictions placed on them to truly shine. I found myself too frustrated to be scared, and so I just stopped playing.

208623034.review:
Played on hard difficulty and it certainly felt hard for most of the game. The gameplay is very survival horror with you scavenging for the meager amounts of resources available on the increasingly desolate Sevastopol space station while dealing with its hostile inhabitants and the infamous Alien. You're discouraged from combat for a large amount of the game as it attracts the attention of the unkillable Alien but the game provides a lot of options to either go in loud or quiet. 
  I think I would like the crafting system, in which you gather several different parts of which different combinations are used for the craftables, if the game didn't limit the amount of parts that can be gathered for most of them to a maximum of 3. The amount of craftables we can hold for most of them is also 3. This scarcity made me play more conservatively with my resources (by being sneakier) but that caused me to hoard and made some parts of the game harder than necessary. I also wish we could've weapon crafted ammunition as well. If the scarcity was intentional to help create tension though, I think it did a good job. Though I had hoarded plenty towards the end, the fact that a few bad encounters could force me to use most of my stuff kept me careful and tense.
  The rng nature of the loot was very annoying but I've always found that the game would always eventually give me more than enough to deal with the situation. It is frustrating, though, to loot a box and get good stuff but then die and loot the same box afterwards and find it empty.
  Expect a lot of simple puzzles and being forced to look for and remember/write down passcodes. No UI will pop up next to the keypads to show you the passcode you found for it. Thankfully, you can expect the codes to be nearby so you don't have to trek back and forth. Objectives may require you to pay attention to the context of the situation (eg. you need to press a button but there's not power so you follow the cables to a generator) 
  AI can be clunky, sometimes they won't notice you when they should or vice versa. Encounters with the Alien are instakills so you will learn quick to play sneakily whenever they're around. Creative thinking is rewarded for avoiding or dealing with enemies. Enemies also don't seem to respawn, before you leave the current area at least. Subsequent visits may possibly spawn new or different encounters, especially as you progress the story. 
  Object interactions can be a bit clunky too, you might need to stare at them from a particular angle, especially for small items. Sometimes you'll kinda teleport a short distance to interact with things. Animations can also be annoyingly slow and can give enemies plenty of time to discover and kill you. Worst experience is with facehuggers, as meleeing them forces you into an animation and it sometimes still kills you, especially if you're too close. 
  I don't remember any particular bugs but I did crash a few times for seemingly no reason since I can't reproduce it. 

  Overall, I liked this game. I haven't played Dead Space (as of yet) but the vibes kinda reminds me of it. I have played Prey though and the gameplay reminds of that somewhat. I assume the lower difficulties just makes the enemies less aggressive and more blind so I highly recommend it as a spooky game even for the more unskilled gamer!

208203833.review:
NO SPOILER REVIEW.

I'm torn. There are many things about this game that are phenomenal, yet many design choices that make me question how or why they were implemented into the game at all. In the end, YES, I think this is a great game, but there are a few things holding it back from being stellar. I do not agree with the consensus that this is a "Top 5 Scariest Game of All Time", but it has its moments. Everyone should read this review and others if they are seriously considering this game.

For starters, "Hard" is the difficulty recommended by the developers. It is the way the game is meant to be experienced. It is definitely challenging, and allows for some close calls and truly cinematic moments. However, there are times when it is outright unfair. As others stated, the Xenomorph will frequently ignore other human NPCs whom make a lot of noise, and sometimes B-line it right to you if you accidentally bump into an object or walk through some sensor (that is clearly part of the setpiece) that gives a small audio cue. Enemies will frequently see you when you are peaking at them from great distances, and sometimes inexplicably a target will move from 99+ meters and go directly to the locker you are hiding in. Another frustrating cause of death is when you are moving to an objective beneath one of the ceiling vents the Xenomorph travels through, and the split second you walk underneath it, that's when the AI decides to set the Xenomorph there lurking and it instakills you. I understand that his hissing and his drool is meant to signal that he is there, and that these openings should generally be avoided at all times, but when he spawns right as you are walking underneath an unavoidable opening it is frustrating. You will frequently be redoing sections that will become tedious because of these unfair deaths, which hurts the scare factor quite a lot due to overexposure of the Xenomorph.

With that said, did I lower the difficulty? No. These deaths, while unfair, taught me something: I was at fault for putting myself in that situation in the first place. The game told me that "Combat attracts the Xenomorph", and it does. I have used human in-fighting to attract the Xenomorph to kill groups of human enemies. What the game DIDN'T tell me, that I only learnt through trial and error, was the insane bias the Xenomorph has towards the player. Thus, I took this into account with every encounter moving forward, and found success. Same with the overhead vents: the game TOLD ME the Alien was there with his hissing and drool; what it DIDN'T tell me was how sometimes he would instantly spawn on one right as you were walking beneath. Thus, I tried to avoid these EVERY SINGLE TIME, no exceptions. And, as such, I found success. This is just a few examples, but the game is very harsh with how it teaches you its rules. There are the rules it tells you outright, and then the rules you only learn through trial and error. While I can appreciate this, some players might get frustrated from this, and I understand the reviews where this is a criticism.

My other main gripes with the game are the runtime, the jank, and the real time button inputs. The runtime is an easy discussion: the game is just too long. It overstays its welcome. Players do not feel this way because the game is that terrifying, and a part of us just wishes the torment would end: no, I seriously mean the game is just too long, and becomes tedious. You begin to notice patterns. Its a lot of the same thing over and over again. The setpieces are phenomenal, and the enemy encounters make it worth it, but there is a lot of extra padding that kills the pacing, and ultimately the scares. This is bad mostly for exposure. If you are like me, the game might start off as a horror game first and foremost, but about halfway through it mostly becomes a stealth game. A good rule of thumb for emotional engagements (including things outside of gaming) is to end things on a high note- and I felt there were many opportunities here that the game just did not take advantage of, and for whatever reason just kept going. As a result the player comes down from these highs and their emotional investment begins to dwindle.

The jank is another thing. The game is visually stunning but sometimes it feels like Ripley is more of a Nutcracker doll than she is a human being. Everything she does is in slow motion. I understand this is intentional to add tension but sometimes it doesn't feel so nice to control. For example, all of the animations for using her tools, going in and out of lockers, and more, is just a little too over the top and broke the immersion a little for me.

Lastly, my BIGGEST issue with the game by far are the real time button inputs. Completely unnecessary. Got me killed more times than I can count. Not because I pressed the wrong buttons, but because it adds needless padding and frequently the game does not register player inputs (especially RMB). For example, the one that drives me nuts is the one that initiates when you turn on a generator. Ripley holds a yellow lever and has to pull on it 3 times. Now, tell me, if she just has to simply pull this lever down in one smooth motion, why do I have to mash the E button 6 times? Is she mentally preparing for the lever pull? Is she working up the courage to do something so crazy as pulling down on a lever? And why do I have to do it 3 times? Not only this but it feels like every door and interactive object in the game has some weird combination of buttons that you must hit in a certain order each time, and when you factor in the fact that the game frequently ignores your inputs, it is a recipe for disaster. I was genuinely getting more frustrated with these than anything else in the game by far.

There is also an enemy that will instakill you that is very hard to see, and you only get a few seconds to react to it when it appears. That was something else that I thought could have used more polish.

However, all of that said, this is still a great game, and for fans of the genre or franchise, you will get enjoyment out of this. The art direction and sound design are out of this world. Graphically the game is just insane. And when you do have those "moments", you will remember the fact that the majority of events in this game are unscripted, and I promise you that you will be blown away. 

Take these things in mind and give the game a shot. At the end of the day it's fun, and if any of the above resonates with you on your playthrough, you can always lower the difficulty. My biggest pieces of advice are to plan your routes and strategy in advance, and save frequently. Go out of your way to save points that might be a little off your path. Trust me, it will save you a lot of time and energy.

Hope this helps

*Edit* Just realized I beat this game exactly 9 years ago on the same day I bought it. 11.02.2016. Funny coincidence

208184947.review:
Positives:
- The xenomorph is definitely the best thing about this game. Creative Assembly frickin ace'd it. Every time I interacted with the alien it was just pure dread and terror followed by immense relief when I managed to get past it. Since you are so much weaker than the alien, every time that you outsmart it or gain a new way to deal with it feels incredibly satisfying.

- This game was uniquely challenging. It has a system where the xenomorph adapts to your playstyle over time, which means that you can't use the same tricks over and over to escape or hide from the alien. In order to succeed, you really gotta utilize the game's entire toolkit to get around all of the different enemies. I found this to be really rewarding after I figured it out.

- Aesthetically speaking, I just can't believe how accurate it is to the first Alien movie from 1979. I recently watched it for the first time after playing this game and while watching, I couldn't stop thinking "OMG, that looks exactly like how it is the game!" There are so many references and areas throughout Sevastopol station that makes you feel like you're on the Nostromo. And for being over a decade old at this point, Alien: Isolation' visuals are AMAZING, and performance-wise it holds up well. I didn't experience any crashes or bugs in my first play through.

Negatives:
- The manual save points were infuriating. Every time that you try and save, it takes forever and then pauses the game and asks you "Are you sure you want to overwrite your current save data?" I'm pretty sure I saved like over 50 times in my first play through and not a single time did I sit there and think "hmmm nah I actually don't want to save!" Another thing about this mechanic is that if you manually save and the alien just so happens to be really close to you, every time that you load back to that save point, you will die instantly to the alien. This happened to me on several occasions and it was SO infuriating because the only way to fix it was to go back to a previous save and lose all my progress. I will say, I liked the little blinking noise the save point makes to indicate when its nearby, that was a nice touch that saved me many times.

- I found the looting/crafting system to be a little wonky. In my first play through, there were a couple items that I only crafted once or twice (notably the molotov and emp mine) because I just never had enough resources to make them. 

- The map was super confusing at times. There's a green circle (that indicates where the next objective is) which shows up on the map regardless of what floor you're on. For me this lead to a lot of confusion. There were times where I would just be aimlessly walking around the map trying to figure out where I needed to go to no avail. I would have appreciated a 3D map or maybe some visual indicators on screen to point you where you have to go.

Ultimately I really enjoyed this game and I highly recommend it! Probably the best survival horror game I've ever played.

208072635.review:
Alien: Isolation isn’t just a horror game — it’s an ordeal, in the best possible way. From the moment you step aboard the decaying space station Sevastopol, the atmosphere grips you by the throat and doesn’t let go. Every flickering light, every metallic groan, and every hiss of steam is a reminder that you’re not alone — and that thing stalking the corridors is far more patient than you are.

What makes the game so brilliant is its respect for the 1979 film. The art direction is spot-on — that chunky, analog-future tech; the CRT screens; the hum of the ship’s systems — it feels authentic, lived-in, and claustrophobic. The sound design deserves its own award: the motion tracker’s beep can make your heart rate double on its own.

The Alien AI is the real star. It learns. It hunts. It punishes complacency. You can’t just memorize patrol routes or hide in lockers forever — it adapts, and that unpredictability is what keeps the tension alive for all 20+ hours. Few games sustain that level of dread without fatigue, but Isolation somehow does.

If there’s one flaw, it’s pacing. Some segments drag near the middle, and the game occasionally mistakes “stress” for “fun,” stretching encounters longer than they need to be. The android “Working Joes” are also more annoying than frightening after a while.

Still, those gripes pale next to what Creative Assembly achieved: a survival horror experience that feels genuinely cinematic. It’s not just one of the best Alien games ever made — it’s one of the finest horror games of the decade.

Verdict: 9/10 — Terrifying, authentic, and unrelentingly tense. A must-play for anyone who loves fear served cold and sterile.

208043557.review: being at the brink of pissing myself every five minutes was very fun used about 2 of the 7 hours playing this game in the bathroom

208021918.review:
[h1]Alien: Isolation[/h1]
[h3][Overview][/h3]
Alien: Isolation is a 2014 survival horror game by Creative Assembly. You play as Amanda Ripley, daughter of Ellen Ripley, still haunted by her mother's disappearance after the Nostromo went dark. Amanda gets word that the flight recorder might've turned up, so she hops a ride to Sevastopol Station as part of the recovery team. Things go sideways fast, the crew is scattered, and now Ripley must survive on her own.

[h3][Recommended Tweaks & Tips][/h3]
- [url=https://github.com/ThirteenAG/AlienIsolation.SkipSaveConfirmationDialog]Skip save confirmation dialog.[/url]
- [url=https://www.nexusmods.com/alienisolation/mods/34]Enhanced graphics settings (Optional)[/url]
- Play on Hard difficulty. Items and save points are still plentiful, but gameplay is more tense and dangerous.

[h3][Positives][/h3]
- Great graphics and art style. The visuals and lighting remain beautiful to this day.
- Intense, strategic gameplay. Hard mode ramps up the strategy, making the Alien smarter and deadlier.
- Immersive. Strong atmosphere and audio design pulls you into the world, adding to the horror.
- Good level design. Maps are designed with multiple routes, making stealth more dynamic. Some vents are accessible by both the player and the Alien, adding a sense of paranoia and lack of true safety.
- The game rewards risk-taking and bold decisions.
- Great sound design. Alien’s footsteps are weighty, and its hisses are horrifying.
- Good music. It’s not grand, but it adds to the atmosphere.
- Hardcore, unforgiving stealth mechanics. Mistakes are punished heavily, as the Alien is faster than you and will kill you in one attack, increasing stakes and tension.
- Good weapon/gadget variety. Each weapon and gadget feels unique and serves its own purpose.
- Attention to detail. Details include the Molotov and Flamethrower emitting light that’s visible to enemies, the Alien’s skull being visible under the right light conditions, many small nods and references to the films, etc.
- Decent flashback missions. They’re fairly slow and linear, but they help break up the pacing a little.
- Faithful addition to the Alien franchise. The game is very authentic to the films and fits right in.

[h3][Mixed][/h3]
- Slow movement mixed with large areas makes traversing a little frustrating at times.
- Synthetics are disappointing enemies. They aren’t really scary, they’re more of a frustrating nuisance than anything.
- Alien’s AI effectively cheats and rubber-bands close to your location. This does add tension, but can also feel a bit cheap.
- The Molotov and flamethrower are solid weapons, but they’re a bit too powerful. They both fully stop the Alien in its tracks and force it to retreat, undermining the horror and difficulty.
- Disappointing ending. It’s not terrible, but it’s very abrupt and provides little to no closure.
- The dialogue’s writing-quality feels inconsistent at times. The voice acting is also spotty.
- Mildly annoying quick time events. They sometimes add tension, but do we really need to remember three different button combos for pulling levers?
- The game's low render distance leads to occasional pop-in (Use the Enhanced graphics settings tweak to fix.)

[h3][Negatives][/h3]
- Length. The game is too long and repetitive, mechanics start to feel samey by the halfway mark. Backtracking and long walking segments add filler.
- Unfair deaths. The Alien will sometimes spawn right in front of you, leading to immediate deaths.
- Low enemy variety. There are only four enemy types: Xenomorphs, synthetics, humans, and less frequently, facehuggers. More enemy variety would make gameplay less repetitive and predictable.
- Mildly buggy. Random objects spawning midair, getting stuck falling in a vent, tram cars spawning in invisibly, etc.
- Save confirmation dialog is unnecessary and immersion breaking (see the Recommended Tweaks.)
- PC issues. 100+ FPS breaks certain sound effects, 60+ FOV makes the player model invisible, subpar anti-aliasing, forced mouse smoothing, keyboard controls are unintuitive, etc.
- Lackluster and overpriced DLC. Most of them are incredibly short.

[h3][Conclusion][/h3]
Immersive and intense, Alien: Isolation is a great horror game. It’s too long and has too little variety for its own good, but it makes up for it with horrifying gameplay and great design.
9/10

207985157.review:
I don't think the words I could give would properly give the game the justice it deserves, but I'm gonna try my best. Alien Isolation is a modern horror classic plagued by review bombs upon launch. 11 years later at the time of this review, this game holds up extremely well for a game that was released in 2016.

This game, many years later, tackled adapting the Alien franchise into being scary once more, and put aside the "run and gun" and shoot 'em up game style, and opts in for a more tactical and stealth approach. Stealth is a primary focus of the game, trying to avoid humans, the alien, and other nuisances. The game has a huge focus on atmosphere. Every sound of the Nostromo, every sound you make, every sound a human makes, every sound the alien makes, it is all fine tuned to fill you with such dread and anxiety to the point you'll be paranoid about "Where is the alien gonna drop? Oh god what was that sound?! I'm doomed, aren't I." I don't hear the sound design of the game get talked enough, actually. This game immerses you into the character you control, every sound you hear is designed to make it feel as if you, the player, are experiencing the horrors the main character has to face and endure, and it is all delightful. Even the moments where you have to be out in the vacuum of space are such a sweet treat, listening to the metal clash against the Nostromo, the absolute dead air leaving you only to imagine what is happening around the ship.

I would recommend a first timer's play through of this game to be on Hard mode so they always feel pressured by the Alien and the enemies the game throws at you. Alien Isolation is what I like to describe as a game that not only challenges you, but also tests your ability to adapt and find ways to deal with each individual challenge, but the game also, unfortunately, suffers from an issue where if you play on lower difficulties, you will rarely find challenge, and imo, satisfaction. Humans are dumber, the Alien's stalking and aggressiveness tone down alot, and generally the whole balance of the game is thrown to wack on difficulties on anything below Normal. I believe if you wish to truly experience Alien Isolation, then try the game on Nightmare.

Please do give the game a try, for it is, in my honest opinion, one of the best horror games in modern time, and something I believe alot of horror games fail to recreate what truly causes fear and anxiety. That, is Alien Isolation. Fear and anxiety, incarnated.

207751458.review: "Alien: Isolation" is a true gem and absolutely a unique game in many ways. It combines elements of horror and first-person action-adventure with immersive sim mechanics. While being set in a famous universe, it doesn’t contradict the films - in fact, it enhances and completes the story told in Alien and Aliens. Actually, there are now Alien, Aliens, Alien: Romulus, and Alien: Isolation, and together they create a consistent storyline that respects each other’s plots and ideas. For me, the only correct approach is to ignore all the other Alien movies and consider Aliens the true finale -you know, just like with Terminator 2 and the rest of the Terminator series. There’s also "Alien: Earth", but that’s a strange beast indeed. It ends on a cliffhanger and probably takes place in its own separate universe. And personally, I still see the Alien movies as part of the AvP universe, because I love both Alien and Predator franchises. Speaking of the game itself - I’m not a big fan of horror games and usually prefer gameplay with proper combat systems, action, and so on. But I absolutely enjoyed "Alien: Isolation" because it’s made very differently from, say, "Outlast", while still staying true to the horror genre. Many players couldn’t even finish it because of how intense it is! Despite being released in 2014, it still holds up remarkably well in terms of graphics and gameplay. The Xenomorph hunts you relentlessly throughout the Sevastopol station - and its behavior is not scripted. The developers created two AIs: one is the Xeno’s own AI, which doesn’t actually know where the player is, and the other is the “director AI,” which does know and occasionally hints your location to the Xeno’s AI. So, to survive, you have to improvise and think strategically. Hiding in lockers won’t save you - the Alien will search, and eventually it will find you. Movement and adaptation are your only ways to survive. And then there’s the cherry on top - the art direction. The Sevastopol station is one of the best fictional sci-fi locations ever created, in my opinion. It’s designed as if it were built for a film made in 1979 or the early 1980s. The developers absolutely nailed that retro-futuristic aesthetic, and even the cast of the original movie was so impressed that they agreed to participate in the project. So yes - you’ll hear all their voices, including Sigourney Weaver herself! If you ask me, I definitely recommend "Alien: Isolation" even today. However, as great as the game is, it’s not perfect. The last two levels feel a bit rushed, like a typical endgame segment from the Xbox 360/PS3 era, and the ending is quite strange. Obviously, I’m very glad that a sequel is now officially in development. Hopefully, it will retcon those odd digital series and reveal that everything was just Amanda’s bad dream at the end of the game.So, to conclude this review I would like to say that "Alien: Isolation" is one of the best games I ever played!

ABSOLUTE. FUGGIN. CLASSIC. I will NEVER forget the first time I played Alien: Isolation on my Xbox 360, all of those years ago. Compared to Aliens: Colonial Marines, this game delivered something truly special after the dumpster fire that was Colonial Marines. It wasn't a cheap cash grab, and it was crafted with passion from the developers. It is actually cannon to the film franchise, and overall it has held up so well despite being released back in 2014. Playing as Amanda Ripley, Ellen Ripley's daughter had me on my feet with each and every minute I spent playing. The soundtrack is phenomenal, the story and script are solid, the atmosphere and immersion break the scale and goes above a 10/10 scale for me personally. To this day I still experience anxiety, paranoia, and goosebumps when I play this game. It is nearly impossible to predict the Aliens behavior during game-play due to the care and passion behind the team from Creative Assembly, and it is literally the perfect organism. The androids add to the overall horror as they are ruthless, and in some sections will work together with the Alien for the singular goal of ending your life. I still can't believe that IGN only gave this game a 5.9/10, as some, if not most modern horror games released today cannot even COMPETE with this title. If you crave a truly good and terrifying experience that will leave you sweating and your heart pounding, PLEASE play this game. 

It deserves all of the love us fans have showered upon it. 10 grueling long years after it's original release, we are now getting a sequel! I can't wait to see what comes next. 15/10. Absolutely *perfect* video game.

206902672.review:
Alright, listen up, because I’m only gonna say this once. I just played this game — Alien: Isolation — and, uh… I think I need therapy now. And not the soft kind with a clipboard and a candle — I’m talkin’ cryo-freeze me and hope my brain resets, man!

This game? It's not just scary — it's psychological warfare dressed up in retro-future wallpaper. You spend the whole time sneakin’ around a broken-down space station, tryin’ to stay quiet, stay low, and stay alive. Which sounds easy until you hear that first thud in the vents. And then you realize — oh no — this ain’t no ordinary bug hunt. There’s one alien. Just one. And it’s smarter than half my squad ever was.

The thing learns, man. You hide in a locker too long? It figures that out. Use the same noise maker trick twice? Dead. Run? Dead. Breathe too loud? Still dead. It doesn’t play fair — it plays like it wants to watch you panic.

And that’s not even talkin’ about the androids — the Working Joes. These skin-peelin’, red-eyed, polite little psychopaths walk around like they’re helpin’ you, but all they really want is to choke the life outta you while apologizin’. That’s just insult to injury, man. “You always know a working Joe,” they say. Yeah? Well I always know a nightmare when I see one.

But here’s the kicker: I couldn’t stop playin’.

Every time I died — and trust me, it was often and loud — I’d reload and crawl back in like an idiot, hopin’ this time I’d be smart enough or quiet enough or lucky enough to make it to the next damn save station. Which, by the way, are about as rare as a sane Weyland-Yutani exec. And don’t even think about quicksaving — nah, this game don’t care about your comfort. You want to live? You earn it.

The world feels real. Like, dangerously real. The station's falling apart in the most beautiful, miserable way. CRT monitors, hissing doors, flickering lights — it’s all got that greasy 70s space-tech vibe that makes you feel like you’re sittin’ in the original Nostromo crew lounge, waitin’ to get snatched through the floor.

And the sound? Oh, man… the sound. Every drip, every rattle in a vent, every footstep — you second-guess everything. You’ll spend ten minutes starin’ at a motion tracker beepin’ like your heartbeat while wonderin’ if that dot on the screen is death or just a rogue coffee cup.

By the end of it, I wasn’t even playin’ a game anymore. I was Amanda Ripley. I was her fear, her exhaustion, her stubbornness. I felt it. And yeah, I screamed. Loud. Once in front of the mirror. Not proud of that.

So yeah, final word? This game is a masterpiece. It’s mean, it’s tense, it’s unforgivin’ as hell — and I respect the hell outta it. It doesn’t hold your hand. It slaps it away, throws you a wrench, and says, “Survive.”

And somehow, that’s exactly what I wanted.

206734958.review:
If jump scares work on you, you'll probably love this game. Jump scares very rarely ever work on me, so I didn't find this game scary in the slightest. Atmospherically creepy at times in the beginning, sure, but I just don't understand how this game terrifies people. 

Alien: Isolation is a stealth game with puzzles. I found using my eyes and ears far more useful than the practically useless motion detector. The xenomorph is pretty easy to evade by simply walking slowly and ducking under something when it's close. There was one mission, chapter 7 or 8 I think, where the xenomorph drops from a light, that I took slow and it nailed me close to the end of the chapter. I already knew where to go, so I tried sprinting the whole chapter on my retry. It worked. It didn't work on the next chapter, but I only had to hide once and it was still pretty simple and straightforward. All the missions were if you use your map.

So I asked myself a simple question after 5 hours of playing what's basically a walking simulator with some hiding and light puzzles: Am I having fun? Honestly, no. I don't think it's scary and I find the game play tedious. Your mileage may vary, but I wouldn't recommend this game to friends and I'm so glad I bought it at a deep discount.

For the most immersive and pulse-pounding experience, I highly recommend playing on Hard mode. Not only does it amplify the suspense, but it also rewards you with a dedicated trophy.

An astoundingly atmospheric game thanks in large part to a thrilling soundtrack.
If this game was half as long it would be a succinct accomplishment, however, it drags on with no real meaning; most of the time it felt like a crawl-paced walking simulator.
I played on medium as it felt the most like the "intended" experience of the game, however, I regret this as the game was far too easy. I do wonder if this difficulty made this feel as much of a waiting game as it did. Maybe if you played hard or nightmare you would "have to keep moving".  I have found out after playing that apparently the game recommends hard, but I missed that and I am quite annoyed :(
I think this game, on the whole, is not worth 15 hours of your time, but it is certainly not a bad game. I did find a lack of polish (eg buggy event queues, sound balance & sound triggers, animation glitches) which do remove immersion, yet any immersion was already extinguished by the fetch-quest walking simulator the second half of this game peters in to. Perhaps if I didn't need to pull up my map every five seconds to be sure of where I was going this wouldn't have torn my immersion. I'm either stupid or it was not intuitive

The sound design and music of this game is among the most impressive I've ever seen, yet the gameplay and story let it down and, disappointingly, there was soon nothing to fear

205743058.review:
When I first picked up Alien: Isolation on Xbox back in 2014, I wasn’t prepared for just how terrifying it would be. From the moment you step onto Sevastopol Station, the game makes you feel vulnerable and hunted. Unlike so many horror titles that rely on cheap jump scares, Isolation thrives on atmosphere, tension, and the terrifying unpredictability of its xenomorph AI. Streaming it on Twitch was almost as intense as playing it—the chat could feel my nerves every time the motion tracker blipped.

What makes this game unforgettable is the sense of dread it builds. The xenomorph isn’t scripted—it learns, it stalks, and it adapts. That unpredictability made every playthrough unique. Add in the retro-futuristic design faithful to Ridley Scott’s original Alien, the creaks and groans of the station, and the sheer helplessness of hiding under a desk while hearing heavy footsteps above you—Isolation nails survival horror in a way few games ever have.

I’ve beaten it multiple times on both Xbox One and PC, and each run still gets my heart racing. The game isn’t perfect—it can be long, and the androids (“Working Joes”) sometimes overstay their welcome—but the highs are so strong that they overshadow any flaws. Alien: Isolation remains, to me, one of the most genuinely terrifying games ever made, and one of the best adaptations of a film license into a video game.

A masterclass in survival horror. I can not wait for the sequil !
