205648701.review:
There is a common problem that horror video games generally face. The ability to surprise the player and retain the horror when all of its tricks has been revealed. A horror game will inevitably become less scary, when all of its quirks have been unveiled, when the monsters have been documented, and the location of keys are mapped. When expectations are set in stone it becomes harder to scare and be surprised.
A magician never reveals all of his secrets for how else would he continue you to trip you up? Alien Isolation might feel like it shows its hand once you’ve gleaned a single playthrough, but the truth is that the relentless and surprisingly smart AI of its merciless Xenomorph means that no single playthrough will ever be the same. And the intelligence of the Xenomorph means that I could never let my guard down for a second.

Creative Assembly had done something truly remarkable, they’ve managed to adapt a beloved movie IP unto video games and evoked the claustrophobic retro-future vibes of Ridley Scott’s original film with extraordinary attention ro detail. The scenery, the constant chirping of computers and terminals will take you right back to the 1980’s charming retro vision of the future seen through such films as Ridley Scott’s Blade Runner and of course Alien.

The sound design, the lighting, it all resonates with the startlingly smart enemy AI to create a very unique and timeless horror experience. It keeps you on edge because you need to be on constant watch for the relentless killer that pursues you throughout the experience, which only gets more tense when the world around serves to distract you from the fact that there is dangerous killing machine stalking every nook, cranny and vents specifically to kill you. While trying to keep track of its movement using a motion sensor you also have to contend with other hazards on the station that keeps tension high.

The Xenomorph has a reputation for being one of the scariest monsters to grace cinema, so it’s only appropriate that it retains this visage for video games. In Alien Isolation the alien learns from how you avoid its presence, it will see through you if you try to repeat tactics to outsmart it, if you try to hide in the same places it will think to look there. And it of course responds to noise, and naturally it isn’t the only obstacle you will have to deal with onboard the Sevastopol space station and as such like any smart killer it will take advantage of your carelessness if you let it. The presence of hostile humans can also prove to be a boon for you to distract thr alien while you accomplish your task of fixing generators and make your slow way back to your ship to evacuate the death trap you’ve found yourself in.

You play as Amanda Ripley, daughter of Ellen Ripley protagonist of Alien who is traveling to the space station Sevastopol based on a tip given to her by her synthetic companion Samuels that the flight recorder of the Nostromo, her late mother’s ship, has been recovered. Seeking closure for her missing mother she decides to take a job to Sevastopol, which unfortunately puts yet another Ripley into contact with the terrifying Xenomorph and the corporate evil of Weyland-Yutani. Amanda has all the characteristics of her mother, her disposition feels very grounded and genuine for a civilian engineer being put into a life or death situation involving a killer alien monster, rogue Synthetics and crazed human survivors.

Alien Isolation thankfully avoids being the type of horror game where you spent all of your time hiding. The Alien cannot really be defeated, because of course not it is simply too strong and resourceful to be taken down like a common Resident Evil Licker. But it can be stopped. The game allows you ways of pushing the monster back, or distracting its attention unto other matters to give you time enough to push forward with your objective. When you get the flamethrower, the Hunter becomes the hunted!
More often than not your objective involves restarting some generators like an average AAA video game, thankfully the tension is at a constant high that it never gets boring.. Well, at least after the Alien shows up. The beginning hour of the game takes a bit of time before it ramps up, and the last hours of the game felt like the game refused to end where it probably should.
Looking past that, Alien Isolation is an all time brilliant survival horror game and a landmark achievement for horror games that I won’t soon forget. Now, bring on the sequel Creative Assembly!

205477686.review:
[h1]Review After 100% Completion[/h1]

[h2]Recommendation: Strongly Recommended[/h2]

[h3]What I Liked (Pros):[/h3]
+ The atmosphere is incredible, every hallway, vent, and flickering light feels like it’s pulled straight from the original Alien film. It’s tense, immersive, and constantly keeps you on edge.
+ The Alien is genuinely terrifying. Its unpredictability and presence creates constant dread, and it never feels scripted.
+ Sound design is phenomenal. From the distant thuds in the vents to the beeping of the motion tracker, audio plays a huge role in building suspense.
+ Stealth gameplay is rewarding. Staying hidden, timing your movements, and using distractions feels satisfying when it all comes together.
+ Visuals are stunning. The retro-futuristic design of Sevastopol nails the aesthetic of Ridley Scott’s original vision.
+ The story is compelling. Playing as Amanda Ripley, trying to find her mother, adds emotional weight and ties beautifully into the franchise.
+ [spoiler] The flight recorder flashback sequences with the original Nostromo crew finding the derelict engineer ship on the planet for the first time and seeing all the alien eggs just like in the first movie, so good. [/spoiler]
+ The second half of the game was a complete surprise, [spoiler] discovering a full hive and multiple fully grown Xenomorphs was insane. [/spoiler]
+ The ending was powerful and left a lasting impression.

[h3]What I Didn’t Like (Cons):[/h3]
- Some mechanics feel inconsistent, like being spotted through walls or vents when you thought you were safe.
- Alien encounters can feel unfair, especially when it drops down randomly right next to you.
- Facial animations are stiff and occasionally break immersion during dialogue scenes.
- Combat with androids (Working Joes) feels clunky and underwhelming compared to the rest of the game’s polish.
- Backtracking through previously cleared areas can slow the pacing and feel repetitive.

[h3]Wishlist – Things I’d Love to See Added:[/h3]
[list]
[*] Roguelike Mode – A randomised survival mode with different layouts, threats, and objectives would make replaying even more exciting. The unpredictability of the Alien would shine in this format.
[*] Expanded Crafting System – More depth in crafting and upgrades could add variety to how you approach each encounter, especially in later missions.
[*] More Alien Variety – The hive reveal was incredible. Seeing different types of Xenomorphs or mutations would add even more tension and surprise.
[*] Improved Combat Feedback – Melee and weapon interactions with Working Joes could use more polish to match the rest of the game’s responsiveness.
[*] Environmental Storytelling – More subtle clues, logs, and visual storytelling would deepen immersion and reward exploration.
[/list]

[h3]Achievements:[/h3]
A few achievements are easy to miss, most collectables can be picked up later through backtracking, but some require very specific conditions, like avoiding human kills or staying completely unseen in certain missions. A few that come to mind are:
[list]
[*] One Shot – Complete the game without dying. Easily the hardest achievement, but you can avoid death by quickly pausing and reloading your last save.
[*] 100 Times Too Many – Get killed by the Alien 100 times. This was especially grindy after doing a no-death run first, had to intentionally walk into the Alien over and over.
[*] The Taken – Collect all 50 ID tags. Some are tucked away in obscure corners and require serious exploration.
[*] My Turn Now – Kill an android using only the maintenance jack. Tough to pull off without getting wrecked.
[*] Hide. Run. Survive. – Complete the fifth mission without being killed by the Alien. Sounds simple, but the tension makes it brutal.
[*] Mind Your Step – Navigate Reactor Maintenance without dying. [spoiler] Facehuggers, [/spoiler] water, tight spaces, and the Alien all make this a nightmare.
[/list]

[h3]Memorable Quotes:[/h3]
[list]
[*] "Something's On This Station. Something You Wouldn't Believe."
[*] "It's Called Surviving."
[*] "Running causes accidents."
[*] "Follow me, please."
[*] "I Had To Get It Off The Station! By Any Means Necessary."
[/list]

[h3]Final Thoughts:[/h3]
I started playing this after watching Alien: Earth because that show has been an amazing return to form for the Alien franchise, and I’m glad I did, this is easily the best space survival horror game I’ve played. The Alien’s AI is absolutely wild. It’s actually two AIs working together, a “Director AI” that always knows your location and a “Creature AI” that doesn’t. The director feeds hints to the alien, guiding it toward you without letting it cheat. It creates this terrifying illusion that the alien is learning and adapting to where you hide, and it works so well.

[spoiler] The second half of the game completely blew me away. I genuinely didn’t expect there to be a full hive and multiple fully grown Xenomorphs. Not sure how I managed to avoid that spoiler, but everyone always talks like there’s only one alien, which is far from the truth. [/spoiler] 

I’ve loved the Alien franchise since I was a kid, and it’s amazing how well this game captures the tone and style of the original film. It genuinely feels like you’re playing an extension of Alien, the pacing, the visuals, the tension, it’s all there. Playing as Amanda Ripley, uncovering the truth of the Nostromo, and surviving the horrors of Sevastopol was unforgettable. And that ending? Loved it.

It’s absolutely wild that IGN gave this a 5.9 “mediocre”? That dude should be ashamed. This game only got better and better as you went through the story. Bring on the sequel.

205266443.review:
**Alien: Isolation Review - 9/10**

Alien: Isolation is easily one of the best horror games I've ever played. The 80s aesthetic is absolutely perfect, from the chunky computer terminals to the industrial design of the space station. Every detail screams authenticity to Ridley Scott's vision, and it creates this incredible sense of immersion that pulls you right into that universe.

But the real star of this game is the xenomorph AI, and it's genuinely terrifying how smart this thing is. This isn't some scripted monster following predetermined paths—this alien actually hunts you. It learns your hiding spots, adapts to your behavior, and creates this constant sense of dread because you never know where it might be. The AI is so good that I spent most of my playthrough genuinely paralyzed with fear, hiding in corners and hoping against hope that the alien wasn't somewhere nearby. I'm not exaggerating when I say this game took me forever to finish because I was too scared to move most of the time.

The pacing of tools and equipment feels really well thought out too. You get new gadgets and weapons at just the right moments to match the story beats, and everything feels purposeful rather than just handed to you. The motion tracker becomes this incredible source of both relief and terror—you need it to survive, but that blip on the screen might be the last thing you ever see.

What really got to me was the ending. Without spoiling anything, it leaves you with this heavy sense of existential dread. The xenomorphs are still out there, waiting, and there's this crushing realization that humanity is just prey in a universe filled with perfect killing machines. It's the kind of cosmic horror that sticks with you long after you've turned off the game.

Alien: Isolation proves that AI can be the most terrifying enemy in gaming when done right. It respects the source material while creating something uniquely horrifying in its own right. If you want to experience what it's really like to be hunted by the perfect organism, this is essential. Just be prepared to spend a lot of time hiding and questioning every sound you hear.

204038641.review:
If you like the Alien movies, you should definitely play this game. But even if you're not familiar with the movies, you should definitely give this game a chance. 
I had no idea what I was getting myself into and thought the game was a normal space shooter like Halo or Mass Effect. After an hour of playing, however, I quickly realized that this game isn't just about mindlessly slaughtering aliens, but that it's actually a horror game. I was also very surprised by the graphics, as I didn't expect a game that is over 10 years old to be so beautiful.

The aliens, who are completely overpowered at the beginning, feel unbeatable at first. They remain so, but you quickly learn to outsmart them using various techniques and items in order to sneak away unnoticed or simply survive. The overall story is very captivating, and it's interesting to see how the protagonist tries to understand what is happening and how she can escape from an alien-infested ship. 

For me, along with Resident Evil 7, it is the strongest horror game the gaming market has to offer, and I wrongly ignored it for too many years.

204022065.review:
So, this game has been an annual play for me since it was released. I've owned it on several consoles and now on Steam (as well as Epic). 

It is one of my favorite games of all time. I can't overstate how much I adore it. The cool thing is that I tend to learn some new mechanic that's kind of game changing nearly every time I play it. 

Best practices to get the most out of the game:
-Always the hardest difficulty
-Save every chance you get
-Crouch!!!!  
-DON'T RUN!
-Go slow and look every where. Loot is sparse!
-Hide a LOT
-Stay out of lockers, they're finicky
-Play in the dark with headphones
-If you have a mic, turn it on and shut up
-Run away and hide from early working joes. They're bullet sponges and you'll want all your ammo for one specific mission that has a million joes and you really have no choice but to deal with them.    
-Save flame thrower fuel for part of the same mission.
-Save ALL ammo I guess haha You'll know when you actually need it, trust me!
-Take chances....
BUT
-Don't underestimate the xenomorph! 

The game has it's problems without question. The Joe's mostly.. Unfortunately they are the main threat for like half the game and at no point are they as scary as the Xenomorph. They're just annoying and relentless. 

As much as I loathe the Joe's, there's no amount of them that can stop me from loving this game. It is by far the best survival horror I've ever played, and I've played a lot of them. In fact, after you play this, your standards will go up quite a lot and you'll just be looking for games that feel like it. To that I say Good luck... 

Buy it, play it, love it. If you're on the fence, don't worry it's always on sale for like $10 or something. But it is totally worth full retail in my opinion. 

Oh yeah, you can mod it too. Nexus has some pretty cool stuff.

203835591.review:
Its hard nowadays to find games that combine horror, adventure, exploration, and narrative so seamlessly. Often, one feature is heavily sacrificed for another. Alien Isolation is not a perfect game, but it knows what it wants to achieve. I'm not going to mention all the pros of the mechanics, just want to focus on one particular aspect: identity. From the beginning of the game until the end you know, as a player, the type of game you're playing - its a survival horror against enemies that will easily kill you. The environments are realistic in a sense that they can work for or against you equally. The game is fair, and the AI is more aggressive and smarter on higher difficulties. You need to role-play. How would you approach a wide open area knowing that at any moment a alien freak can descent upon you and wreck you. Surely its not going to be sprinting, making noise, and pressing random buttons that may start an alarm. Are you going to blast your way through a crowd of 4 armed outlaws with a slow revolver? Just hide in a locker until they pass! Save your flash-bangs for when you really need them. And so on. You need to have some sense of role-play - unlike MANY other "survival"/"horror" games in which most of them time you're thinking "I would never actually do this but here we go". 

Its very much a love letter to fans of the series with tons of lore, extra reading material, and just the overall level design. Its looks like a place people live and research - there are sleeping areas, kitchens, offices, tunnels, hazardous zones, transit systems, and much more. 

(For a fan of the series, the flashback in level 10 was a beautiful surprise!)

Romulus was enjoyable, but there's something so simple and horrifying about this story that modern renditions of Alien stories refuse to adapt. I've played through a couple of times now, but I played on Novice my first time because I was wildly overwhelmed. It became a bit of a breeze, but I'm not interested in punishing challenges and I also go to therapy so I don't need validation through difficulty settings. 203357632.review: Probably the best survival horror game I've ever played. The encounter design combined with the brilliant intelligence of the Alien's AI is simply masterful in this game. This game effortlessly manipulates your emotions, and will absolutely keep you in utter anxiety and suspense while you're making your way through the game. You will probably start ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ your pants when you hear the Alien drop down from a vent into the middle of a room near you, and you will feel genuine terror while you hide around corners and under desks while it methodically plods around the hallways and rooms as it literally hunts you down. Hearing the Alien roaming around the vents near you does an incredible job of letting you know when you've got a little bit of breathing room to carefully explore your surroundings, but constantly reminding you that are are not safe and that danger can (AND WILL) drop down from the ceiling to kill you at nearly any moment should you get careless. They've struck a near perfect balance of keeping the Alien just as threatening and dangerous throughout the entire game, allowing it to start to notice different types of play patterns and adapt its behavior over time without crossing into the territory of it feeling cheap, getting predictable, or feeling unfair. Contrasted to the anxiety of stress of being hunted by the Alien, it only makes the momentary feelings of relief after you've managed to get to "safe space" like an elevator or finishing a mission that much more psychologically rewarding. Truly a great game and in my opinion, a genre-defining title.

203349811.review:
The sloppiest execution on the stealth-horror genre I've played yet as an avid gamer. 

The levels look fantastic, but are totally impractical, with busy visuals everywhere and overcrowded elements of decor and inconsistent lighting that'll have you scratching your head at how you'll tell an interactible piece of the environment, a mission objective, and a useless setpiece apart. The music, likewise, is excellent as far as I've heard, but the sound design that'll alert you when enemies are near is nonexistent, and multiple superficial bugs will tend to silence sound effects, dragging the experience further down into a feeling of mild inconvenience. The game lacks a lot of polish, with certain obvious quality of life elements that are befuddlingly missing (why do I need to hold a key for one whole second to hide when I'm running from threats? why are levels not designed with the LOS AI in mind, so that I'm not always seen by the threats that I'm hiding from? why does the radial menu not reset my cursor to the center of the screen? why do I have to switch between the keyboard and the mouse to craft objects? why can I not press escape to leave the inventory of whatever I'm looting? and so on).

Stability wise, this game is in an unacceptable state to be paying 40€ for after more than 10 years. 

I remember being mildly compelled to buy the game back when it came out for the TF2 cosmetic it yielded, but playing this after all those years, I'm very satisfied that I didn't cave and pay full price at the time. What an immense disappointment... Shameful display.

[b]HANDS DOWN in my top 3 gaming experiences ever, to this day.[/b]

[h3] This game changed me.[/h3]

[i] I have not been the same ever since. [/i]

I am not sure if this is a spoiler, as I have no idea if this was by design or if I had just gotten lucky, but I will never forget the sheer horror [spoiler] of hearing a sound behind me while hiding in a vent, thinking that surely it can't be the xenomorph, only to turn around and see it rushing at me - mind you, this was close to the end of my playthrough, and up until that point, I had been SURE that the vents are the only place where I am ALWAYS safe in this game. Haven't questioned that for a second, because that's what this game had consistently shown me for the first 20-30h.
[strike] Which, in hindsight, is ridiculous to believe, cause hello, have I watched the Alien movies?? [/strike]

I am still not sure if this was designed like this to give me this false sense of safety only to crush it later, or if I had just been getting extremely lucky all throughout the game till that point (even though I was playing on nightmare mode), but when I tell you this changed me, [i] I mean it. [/i]

Any game I have played ever since, I always stay 110% vigilant in any safe room. Played most of the Resident Evil remakes recently and never for a second could I relax while in any safe room, even though RE has not betrayed me like this. 
 [/spoiler]

[h2] It's been over 5 years and my trust issues have still not recovered. [/h2]

And I low key [b] love that for me [/b] - it adds so much immersion to every game.


[b] Closing notes:  [/b]

If you're on the fence with what difficulty to play on - go with the hardest. [b] Trust me.[/b] It's worth it.
And this is coming from someone who usually plays games on normal difficulty, sometimes even going with the story mode. For this one I ended up accidentally going with the hardest mode, and I'm so glad I did because I actually think that makes the game.

[b]As for the Xenomorph character model,[/b]
[list]
    [*]Breathtaking
    [*]Absolute perfection
    [*]Felt giddy when I first saw him
[/list]


PS. If anyone can confirm if this was how the devs intended, or just a fluke in my personal playthrough, that would be cool!

202884509.review: Alien is probably the best and most iconic sci-fi horror series out there. While they've tried to release games in the past with varying degrees of success, Creative Assembly knocked it out of the park with Isolation. It has it's roots in the original film, where the horror is kicked up a notch. This game has perhaps the most impressive AI I've ever seen in a horror title. The alien that stalks you through Sevastapol Station is a nightmare to hide from. It uses all its tools to its advantage, including the claustrophobic ventilation tunnels you sometimes have to navigate. It can sneak up on you and just when you think you're safe, you hear it drop down from a vent shaft and your heart skips a beat. The story is above average for the Alien franchise and is a welcome addition in learning more about the universe. Meanwhile, the soundtrack succeeds in creating an atmosphere of dread and tension while also making nice nods to the original film's score. Next to Dead Space, this is probably my favorite survival horror title.

202850741.review:
This is exactly what an Alien game should be like and should be the bar for any Alien game after this. Just like the Xenomorph itself, it's a "Perfect Organism".

They absolutely nailed the aesthetic and atmosphere of the original Alien from 1979 and no other game has come close to being this faithful to its original source material. The gameplay is a great combination of survival horror (think Resident Evil, Silent Hill, Dead Space, etc) and also traditional horror as the gameplay style changes on the fly depending on what is required of you at the time. The game takes a couple hours to introduce you to the world but as soon as you hit "that" point it is just fantastic. The Alien itself has some of the best AI in any game ever, if not the best. The way it dynamically moves around the station based on you as a player is genuinely impressive yet terrifying. Alien being my second favourite franchise ever makes this experience so much better as the lore is respected and its so enthralling being able to play in these locations yourself.

A sequel felt unlikely, yet it was announced late last year to be in development by the same developers so I am very excited to hear more about it as they reveal more about it. The sequel to Alien, Aliens, is my favourite movie of all time so I would love to see this exact gameplay style from the perspective of the Colonial Marines. There have been multiple games based on the Colonial Marines but they have typically been action games first, which have been really fun but it would be refreshing to witness how terrifying it is to encounter this life form even as a marine with this sort of game play.

Overall this game is a masterpiece and nails everything it set out to do. The sequel cannot come soon enough. My game time was on Xbox One when it released.

i'm not really going to bother repeating the same points everyone else made (tdlr: this game is a masterpiece), but I do want to share my own perspective.

I'm not really a fan of movies, but I really wanted to do something with the Alien franchise. I already knew the plot of the series from culture and reviews, but I actually wanted to EXPERIENCE it. In a way, Isolation was my savior: if you don't like movies, then this game is a perfect way to get into the world of Alien. It was far more enjoyable BEING a part of the world, rather than watching characters explore it themselves. I completed the game on a pretty low difficulty for my first playthrough, but I do plan on going back with more challenge next time!

It's a wonderful game for fans of the Alien franchise, and it's a wonderful game for people who need an entry point.

202465848.review:
[i]Curator disclaimer: five minutes read - I always wanted to review this game![/i]

[h1]Giger Lives[/h1]

Alien design came from our lord and savior H.R. Giger, but his vibes are disappearing from Alien movies. This game is very important if you understand what the first movie did, since it keeps you on it without the interference of "Predators" or "Colonial Marines." I know it's cool but it has no place here, at least for now. 

But what this does for the Alien universe? [b]Minor spoiler:[/b][spoiler]No big revelations other than suggestive references to draconian corporations. Aliens director's cut even hits differently.[/spoiler]

[h1]10 years old game - Have it aged?[/h1]

After all this time, only now I can see how some details were a lot better. Art and level design is talented and technical---the real star of this game. The retro-futurism is inviting to Alien 1 fans considering the 70s aesthetics. Also the sound FX design plays a major role, and the music boosts it as well.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3551774469

[code]GREAT THINGS ALWAYS LIVES[/code]

1- Atmosphere has aged beautifully. Level and art design is where they focused at when making this game.

2- Plenty of sinister vibes and vile moments. Pristine sound effects and macabre music immerses the mind.

3- I love the item assembling of Alien Isolation. Simple but well thought and fun overall.

4- I don't like cheap jump scares, but in Alien Isolation they're done very well.

5- The game's audio channel turns your ears into a survival tool, specially if using headphones.

6- The voice acting is very professional, but I consider the writing weak and undermines the job.

7- Synthetic "Working Joes" (early low-tech droids) are far scarier than the Alien itself. 

8- All the handling of things; keys, tools, consoles, are satisfying. Its always risky manning them.

9- Hacking, switching, surviving and causing diversion is fun, until the middle-end of the game.

10- Attention to details understands horror, from motion tracker tension to Alien's timely animations.

11- The Alien AI has a curious behavior tree---but sadly it was overrated.

*The Alien may learn as you play. Eg; hide too much in lockers and he'll get to inspect lockers more frequently. Other stuff like that is nice. But... the internet exaggerated things back then. At least it was a successful propaganda to sell the game.

12- The Alien is beautiful and sexy with these long legs. Yes I'm that creepy.

*Do not expect fast action or glory kills, this is not that type of game. Horror gets a slow build-up done little by little until you're submersed before climax engages you. Tension usually results in scary orgasms.

[i]Video about planting - No plot spoiler[/i]
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3551786465

[h1]I hope Creative Assembly and SEGA learned with mistakes[/h1]

The bad things are more visible now as well. From everything, the big negative starts around the 10th mission. The great stuff from before just overstays too much. It abuses so much it almost trashes the game.

[i]"I feel thin, sort of stretched, like butter scraped over too much bread."[/i] - Bilbo Baggins.

[code]THE BAD THINGS UNDERMINING IT - KEY WORD "FILLER"[/code]

1- Each time you run the game you're forced to see the logos and can't skip any of them.

2- Only try the Hard mode in a second run. Some imbalance can disturb your first experience.

*Harder modes is when the Alien shows it isn't really all that smart---harder AI mostly spins the behavior randomly like a barrel of a revolver until it finally shoots.

3- Human AI is awful. They're also filling a role to slow you down with unrealistic dumb patrolling.

4- The station does not save items and even bodies disappears, unlike other survival games.

5- The map pauses the game when you use it, but unclear markings and some bugs can be an issue.

6- You'll see some clipping inside covers and doors. A few types of vents and consoles may bug context keys.

7- The main character won't cast shadows after light sources. Weird since everything else does casts.

[i]Video about a bug. No spoiler.[/i]
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3551779077

8- The story flexes hard to avoid a trope. Good moments takes a lot of time to happen.

9- Game breaking bugs may happen, such as "Follow Samuels to Seegson." I had to reload the mission back.

10- Objectives will become very repetitive, often with and for silly reasons, to the very last second of this game.

*It works like this: "Go there to find the stuff!" You do it since the game is awesome! Only for it to pop: "The stuff is not here! Go back" and then you go back... "Repeat!" and this starts to act up after the 10th level. It becomes overused. A lot of people lost interest, Steam statistics shows only 20% of us ever concluded the game.

11- Things are unnecessarily long after the middle-end part. It wears off and harms the game.

*Around the 16th level even the music starts to play weird, very loud in a bad way (I had to lower it in the options). I think direction rushed something or had a bad changings during production. Even the Face-huggers starts to pop-up so cheaply.

[h1]Final Score: A 7 that forgot to be an 8[/h1]

This review was dedicated to Ryan McCaffrey. He actually gets paid to talk xit 🤣

[b]PTSD story:[/b] I forgot the password for the door. I never had that in a game before: My blood pressure went down. I wanted to win so I ignored sounds coming from the vents and focused---game over was around the corner. I had nowhere to hide after opening it and I saw two humans. I heard a big falling noise so I just rushed away. After finding a save-station I left the game to forget the atrocity I experienced; after flying screams and bodies, I knew they were all mauled because of me.

If you liked this review, thumb and comment [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/27184480/]join my curator page clicking here![/url]

202101114.review:
Alien Isolation is a massively overrated game. I played it quite a while ago and never bothered to write a review, but seeing occasional resurgence in the series and everyone recommending the game, I feel compelled to write this. 
I think, most people who sing high praises of Alien Isolation have never beaten the game, nay, I'd go as far to say most of them might not have beaten the 3rd chapter. And that's the thing- the game feels exceptional in a small dose, you start the game- amazing graphics, soundtrack, absolutely immersive atmosphere......as an Alien fan what more can you ask for? People immediately make videos of how amazing this game is....however, this is not a 5-10 hr horror game, no, this is a 20+ hrs horror game, and as you gradually start taking it more seriously, playing it like an usual video game rather than just a graphics showcase, the game falls apart. It is just way too long with some absolutely horrible mission design, and I assure you, most people never play it so far to reach those sections. The AI will initially seem very smart and scary, but trust me after 10hrs and still 10 more hrs to beat the game, you'll feel more irritated and frustrated than anything. It's not even like the game is too difficult, I think I actually died less than 5 times and I intentionally died a few more times just to check out some secret animations, the game's structure is just plain tedious and boring. The game is not completely bad, its like 6.5-7/10; it's just very heavily carried by it's atmosphere, music, and graphics. The actual gameplay is very weak. I would highly recommend that you get this game only when it's heavily discounted and know this that it is a huge timesink. Don't be fooled by reviewers who just gush out praises of the game. IF you do not believe me, check the steam achievements- 90% people have beat the first chapter but only 20% of them have finished the game. Less than 50% people have even finished half of the game.

202082042.review:
Two things make this an S tier horror game:
1. The space station environment is meticulously faithful to the 1979 movie. Many assets and sound effects are lifted right out of it. The devs were clearly Alien nerds and it shows through in the best ways.
2. The Xenomorph's AI was ahead of its time (and frankly still is). Between PC and PS4 I've played this game 10 times and this is what keeps me coming back. No matter how good you are the alien will still surprise you regularly and I love that.

201602660.review:
I [b]tried[/b] to like it. I love the setting, I love the films, I love the whole theme. But the gameplay and some of the design choices had me irritated from start to finish. I dragged myself through it just I wanted to beat it. And now I have. Finally.

I didn’t enjoy the pointless filler interactions that added nothing to the gameplay. Open a door? LB+LS Down. Pull a switch? LB+RB+both sticks down. Start a generator? Mash the A button to fill a circle three times, just because. It’s tedious. Even once it became muscle memory, I kept asking myself what the point of it was. Even the main character’s grating voice gets tiring at some point.

And somehow it got worse by the end. QTEs thrown in out of nowhere, just for the hell of it.

[b]SPOILERS[/b] below,  I guess:

The tropes were so overused they went from familiar to boring. The falling elevators. The "oops, I fell and hurt myself" moments. The lazy puzzle design:
"I need a keycard to continue—oh look, it's in the same room. What a surprise."
"I need a password—I bet it's on this computer conveniently sitting next to the locked door."
Come on.

The slow motion “space walk” sections were clearly meant to be epic or awe-inspiring, but they just dragged thanks to overly long corridors, gravity mechanics, and stairs (??). It killed the mood.

Even how Ripley interacts with some of the in-game tech feels clunky and pointless. Like the scene where she fiddles with the explosives to free the ship. It just felt like busywork, added to pad the runtime.

This is supposed to be an excellent game, but the plot is mid at best. I might give it another shot in VR someday just to see if it’s as immersive as people say. But I’m never doing another playthrough. Once was enough.

Graphics are quite decent for how old the game is. Unfortunately it doesn't have any semblance of antialiasing, the setting barely does anything, but thankfully nvidia's DSR makes up for it.

201451437.review:
From Awe to Frustration:

At first, I really liked this game.

The opening was impressive: the atmosphere was spot on, you truly felt like you were inside a crumbling space station in the Alien universe. The aesthetics, sounds, and world-building were excellent. Almost reminiscent of the good kind of anxiety you'd get from good old System Shock.

The gameplay loop starts promising: you're trapped in a large station, completing objectives ("missions") while avoiding threats like humans, synthetics, and the Alien itself. You're not a fighter, which makes sense and adds to the tension.

But then the problems begin.

The game repeatedly locks you into sections of the base under thin pretexts, forcing you to reach a goal while evading threats. That would be fine if it were well designed, but it’s not. Save points are sparse, and it's easy to die instantly from something you couldn't predict.

The Alien, in particular, feels like a cheap trick. It roams “randomly” in your section, regardless of logic. There are other people making noise elsewhere, yet it’s always conveniently near you. You have no reliable way to understand its behavior. Hiding doesn't guarantee safety either, which turns "stealth" into coin-flipping. The illusion breaks fast.

That’s the real shame: the atmosphere is amazing, the first few hours are strong, but the core gameplay loop becomes tedious. You repeat the same frustrating cycle: hide, fail, reload, repeat. The unfrequent checkpoints make it worse.

In the end, it’s not a horror game, nor a faithful Alien simulation. It’s a chore.

For comparison, I had more fun with Dark Descent and Fireteam Elite. They were tough, but rarely unfair. Alien Isolation, despite all its polish, ends up as an exercise in frustration.

201447300.review: Great game, surprisingly compelling mechanics, captures the look and feel of 1979 movie sets.  And the audio is fantastic.  Wish I had bought it earlier.

201432659.review:
Hide. Listen. Panic. Repeat.

Alien: Isolation is a game that punishes you for thinking you’re in control. The difficulty is relentless, and that’s what makes it so brilliant. The xenomorph isn’t a scripted enemy — it’s a predator that learns from your mistakes, adapts, and ruthlessly punishes any hint of confidence. You think you’re safe? Wrong. You think you’ve saved? Don’t get too comfortable.

Stealth isn’t a choice — it’s everything. The slightest sound could mean your death, so you’ll spend more time crouching in the dark, holding your breath, than doing anything remotely heroic. Running? Forget it. You’re not fast enough, and the alien’s got your number. Even the crafting system — a rare moment of control — is fraught with tension as you scramble for resources while wondering which vent will be your grave.

I've played on difficulty and it's punishing, but it’s fair. Every mistake feels like it could be your last, and you’ll absolutely scream in panic more times than you can count.

9/10 – The hardest, most terrifying game of cat-and-mouse you’ll ever play.

201380627.review:
[h1]The Alien wants me dead. I just want a save point.[/h1]

[quote]
🔹 [i]At the start:[/i] "Awesome, a survival sci-fi in the Alien universe, I’m gonna explore, loot, and stealth-kill that xenomorph with style."
🔹 [i]At the end:[/i] "I’ve been hiding in a damn locker for 17 minutes, my thumbs are numb from clutching the controller, and I swear I peed myself through my spacesuit. Thanks Alien, you’re one cruel bastard."
[/quote]

[b]✅ What the game does really well (too well, it’s scary):[/b]
[list]
[*] The atmosphere. It’s not just a game, it’s a museum-grade re-creation. You hear flickering neon, see dust on CRT screens, almost smell the stale coffee in the Sevastopol station.
[*] The xenomorph: an AI psychopath. It stalks you, listens, learns from your mistakes, and crashes your life like an unexpected bill.
[*] Amanda Ripley, the protagonist, who manages to be believable, likeable, badass, without flashing six-pack abs or some tragic backstory.
[*] The game is harsh, slow, punishing, no mercy. Basically, it loves you — in its own twisted way.
[*] Special shoutout to the sound design: at this level, it’s not a game, it’s a concert of cold sweats.
[/list]

[b]❌ What the game also does (but hey, gotta fill 20 hours somehow):[/b]
[list]
[*] Sometimes it drags. Yeah, you’ve definitely thought: “Wait… am I seriously back in THIS room again?”
[*] The Working Joes, those nightmare Soviet robots, go from creepy to annoying faster than you can say “sad scientist syndrome.”
[*] The xenomorph sometimes spots you just because… it sensed you’re an overconfident player.
[*] You’ll never look at ventilation ducts the same way again. Even at the gym.
[/list]

[hr]

[b]📝 Conclusion:[/b]
Alien: Isolation is a brilliant game, but not for everyone. It’s slow, demanding, terrifying, and it offers you no favors.
If you like when a game stares you straight in the eye and says: [i]“You think you’re ready?”[/i], go for it.
If you want to blast aliens with a shotgun like Colonial Marines, go play something else. Here, it’s hide-and-seek until death.

[spoiler]
💀 I learned to hack a door with a cardboard-made tool, to breathe silently, and to live with the idea that saving = winning. Thanks Alien. You’re ugly, but I love you.
[/spoiler]

201210895.review:
[h1] I Hate This Game with Every Fiber of My Being [/h1]

Let’s start with the good. For its age, the game still looks fantastic. The atmosphere is top-notch, and while the story isn’t particularly original, it keeps you engaged.

Now, let me explain why, despite all its strengths, this review is negative.

I first tried playing this game when it launched 10 years ago and dropped it before reaching the halfway point. Gave it another shot on Xbox — same result. Recently, I bought it on Steam after watching a few videos about it. I wanted to understand what kept me from finishing it. Now, having finally completed it on my third attempt, I can confidently say: this game is garbage.

Don’t get me wrong — I love horror games. But this one didn’t scare me — it just made me suffer.
Here’s why:

[b]1. The save system is atrocious.[/b]
There are no autosaves after completing objectives. Instead, you have scattered manual save stations. The problem is that on some levels, they are so far apart that dying sets you back 20–30 minutes of progress. That’s not fun — it’s frustrating. On top of that, these save points have a cooldown and can’t be used again right away. Only near the end of the game do save points become more common — which just makes you wonder why the entire game wasn’t like that.

[b]2. The Alien is pure chaos.[/b]
You can’t predict or control it. Even hiding in a locker doesn’t guarantee safety — it might just walk in and kill you. Back to square one. It can spawn behind you, or drop from a vent right above your head. Later in the game, you get a flamethrower that can scare it off, but fuel is limited and doesn’t help much.

[b]Conclusion:[/b]
No matter how beautiful or atmospheric the game is, the awful save system and unpredictable Alien behavior completely ruin the experience. I simply can't recommend it.

201122863.review:
One of the best horror games I’ve ever played. There are no ghosts here, but the sense of true isolation while trying to survive from all sides, whether it’s the alien, the androids, or even other humans is deeply unsettling.

The game features a unique AI system for the alien that, in my opinion, is the best player-hunting mechanic I’ve seen. It actually consists of two AIs: one always knows where you are, and it subtly hints that location to the second AI, which actively searches for you. The longer you stay still, the more accurate those hints become. So basically, you can’t just sit and hide, if you stay in place for around 5 minutes, you will be found. Scary, right? That constant pressure to keep moving ramps up the fear and adrenaline with every step you take while trying to survive. That is what true survival horror should feel like.

Sadly, this level of intensity and smart design has vanished from most modern horror games. These days, most just rely on dumb bots patrolling preset paths, while slowing the player down artificially to create “tension.” But it’s not the same.

That’s why this game stands out as a legendary must-play for any horror fan.

(Only one really annoying bug I ran into: if you alt-tab out of the game and come back, everything turns into slow motion. So make sure to finish anything else first—you’ll want your full focus on this game anyway.)

(I feel a bit weird seeing how few hours I have on record here… but to be fair, I’ve actually played through it more than three times on PS4.)

201118500.review:
A Terrifying Masterpiece – Alien: Isolation Still Stands Tall

Outside of video games, I don’t scare easily—but horror games are a whole different beast. My first dive into the genre was Outlast, and let me tell you, I jumped into the deep end like it was nothing. The first jump scare quite literally launched me out of my chair and onto the floor. Ever since, I’ve been hooked on survival horror—even if it takes me days (or weeks) to get through a single game because of how intense it gets.

For a game released in 2014, it holds up phenomenally well. The visuals are gorgeous and atmospheric, the soundtrack is deeply immersive, and the voice actress for Amanda Ripley? Utterly phenomenal—she brings so much emotion and strength to the role.

What really impressed me right off the bat was the game recommending I play on the hardest difficulty—and so I did. And honestly? It was everything I hoped it would be. Challenging, yes, but not impossible—especially for any novice gamer who's willing to be patient, observant, and maybe scream a little now and then. The added difficulty made every decision feel weighty, every encounter with the Alien a breath-holding gamble.

In this game, stealth is your best friend. You are given ways to defend yourself, but supplies are limited, and making noise comes with deadly consequences. You quickly learn that wasting ammo or charging headfirst into danger isn’t bravery—it’s suicide. This creature doesn’t just go bump in the night... and in the vacuum of space, it's always night.

And the Alien? It's not just a monster—it's a force of nature. As a newcomer to the Alien franchise, I was thrilled to find out a game like this even existed—but what I got far exceeded my expectations. The Alien’s AI is masterfully designed. It's unpredictable, intelligent, and genuinely terrifying. I found myself constantly testing what actions would or wouldn’t trigger it, and watching the creature’s behavior evolve as it hunted me was both fascinating and heart-stopping.

I picked this game up on sale, but after experiencing it, I’d say it’s worth every bit of its full price—and then some. This is survival horror at its finest. 10/10. Highly recommended for fans of the genre or anyone looking to see what true tension feels like.

This may be one of the most highly detailed game I have ever played. 

I'll get the bad out of the way:
1. no shadows. Given the silly level of atmospheric lighting and the like, feels like character shadows wouldn't be that big of a stretch.
2. over exposure to the Xeno in the first few chapters can make it more of a chore than a threat, but overall I would say its still very much a threat.
3. the space walks... tedious. too long. 

none of these are deal breakers however. The sheer level of detail alone makes the game worth it. The story is very much in the tone of the original Alien film. Some nice nods to the film also boosts the games credentials. 

the aliens hunting methods make it absolutely terrifying and this isn't helped by the music. Tense atmospheric scores create a very authentic tone and the jump scare chords create a sense of anxiety second to none. The androids also make for an alarming enemy, with their emotionless sagging faces and monotone vaguely threatening prerecorded phrases make them that much more alarming when they are beating you to death. There are human enemies too, these are made up of the survivors. Mostly people trying to stay alive, there are also those that took advantage of the carnage. 

When playing many questions will be asked, here are some answers to save time working them out,
1: yes the alien heard what you just did.
2: yes the alien saw what you just did.
3: yes that noise is the alien heading your way.
4: No the gun doesn't hurt it.

I can't lie to you about your chances..... but you have my sympathies.

Nope. I came here hoping to play against a smart adapting AI.
What I got was rubberbanding and timers. I was duped.
* Rubberbanding : Every 1 minute or so game tells the alien your general location. So, if alien is patrolling an area you are in and you sneak by it, in less than a minute alien will know that you sneaked by into further rooms. 
* Timers : if you hide in "plain sight" (behind boxes etc) alien will not spend too much time in the same room with you. If you hide in the locker, alien will decide to spend a minute or more walking around and going in&out of the room.

I've been trying unsuccessfully to get through the whole of this game without noping out for years now. I finally completed it the other day so I now feel I can give a good faith review.

This is easily one of the best horror games I've ever played, and it's also one of the strongest entries into the Alien franchise in general. I might even go as far as to say that only the original Alien movie beats it for me.

The game perfectly captures the feeling of being relentlessly hunted by something you can't possibly defeat. It gives you places to hide, but punishes you if stay in them for too long. You start to become sensitive to every little sound that could indicate the alien is nearby.

The visual design is also just great. They fully stuck with the 1970s cassette futurism aesthetic of the original movie and it works really well.

I'll definitely be working my way through the DLCs and looking forward to A:I 2.

200450693.review:
I tried the game three times, but every time had enough of it within an hour...

Aliasing is OUTRAGEOUS! I tried different monitors (I had experience with OLED showing less aliasing in some games), mods, supersampling from 4k, changing settings and gamma, even CRT filter. The game is just unplayable. Heck, there is even aliasing in MOVIE cutscenes. Very little, but still!

I wonder how people aren't seeing it? I get really bad headache from this, so it's surprising nobody mentions it in their recommendations...

P.S you know what I wonder? I play old 3d games like Tomb Raider 1-9 or Fallout 3/NV/Skyrim with no AA what so ever, AND IT DOESN'T BOTHER ME AT ALL. quite the opposite, I prefer the sharp look. So why the hell games from this period (I'm thinking mostly late PS3, early PS4) neither work with AA on nor off? Like in games before and after AA can be toggled on and off for visual customization, but in this time period devs couldn't do either. I would bet that it's because earlier devs were making games with sub 900p resolution, and afterwards they had to think about FSR/DLSS upscaling, so it reflects in how they optimized the polygons and detailisation - while in this period people just discovered 4k and were not thinking too deep about the visuals, as they will always be able to make showcase at super high resolution on the game events, completelly ignoring how it will look when people are playing it....
Anyway, sorry for the rant
upd: I figured out why the aliasing here bothers me so much - it's white and not the colour of surrounding objects. That's why other games with awful aliasing, like nier replicant and automata, look good on oled steam deck, while this one is unplayable for me. Well that and third person view xD

P.P.S. tried the game on switch - and the image is perfect, sharp with no aliasing. Why couldn't they made the windows version baked with good aa? Although the imput lag there makes me unable to play it there either. 30 seconds and I'm ready to burf from motion sickness. seems nothing is perfect...

This is a Video Game Hall of Fame.

This is the Alien Universe Experience.

You are not in control. 

You are the prey.

Every "stalker" game has this as the template or with its design philosophy in mind.

Sound : 5/5 poop your pants
Gameplay : 2 Alien AI in one Xenomorph [1 knows where you are at all times and 1 has no idea]  and as you do things, you will be teaching the AI how to react to you and the omnipotent AI will slowly help the unknowing AI. 5/5. Not all NPCs are hostile... or are they... depending on your actions? Hee hee. Resourse management, deployables,  crafting of tools, upgrades schematics for better tools 
Visuals : 5/5 yes
DLC : Extra challenge modes like the Arkham Batman games, but they expand in story beats, give you different objectives to complete and a timer so don't stay put forever  hee hee. 

Vr: Mother VR mod is available and I played Alien Isolation 2x in VR on Normal and Hard. You can play it in the bathroom for a quick laxative experience if you're stopped up.  Great stuff. Motion Controller support is present. 

If you have a VR, get it.
If you don't have a VR , its like $30 bucks, just get it. 
If you're a person who gets scared easily, this will not be something you can complete in one sitting. It will take you a long time and in some cases,  I don't know if you can play it unless you get a thicker skin. It may tive you nightmares , like he nightmare difficulty with no HUD and a malfunctioning motion tracker. Hee hee 😜

199567799.review:
One Girl, One Xenomorph, and Zero Bullets

Let’s set the tone: I booted up Alien: Isolation thinking I’d be some badass sci-fi action hero, unloading pulse rifles into walls like Ripley in Aliens. What I got was fifteen hours of hiding in lockers, holding my breath, and whispering, “Please don’t see me, please don’t see me,” like a coward in a jumpsuit. And I loved every terrifying second of it.

This is not a run-and-gun power fantasy. This is pure survival horror—and not the polite kind. Isolation is here to remind you that space is cold, the 1970s future is weirdly analog, and you are always one noise away from becoming an alien Happy Meal.

You play as Amanda Ripley, daughter of the Ellen Ripley, and I’ll be honest: I was crushing hard within the first ten minutes. Amanda isn’t a super-soldier. She’s an engineer. A thinker. A survivor. She has that look—tough, determined, and way too smart to be on this derelict space station full of lunatics, homicidal androids, and oh yeah… a 9-foot-tall murder lizard with an attitude problem.

Let’s talk about the alien. Or as I like to call him, Mr. Whispers. He doesn’t follow a script. He isn’t bound by mercy or logic. He just hunts. You’ll hear the thud of a vent, freeze, and pray to synthetic space Jesus that he picks the other hallway. And even when he does, you know he’ll be back in 45 seconds like a nosy landlord checking for noise complaints. It’s terrifying. It’s brilliant. It’s a masterclass in enemy AI.

The game gives you weapons, sure—but they’re mostly symbolic. You’ll find a revolver with six bullets and realize it’s about as useful as a stern look. Ammo is a precious, sacred currency. Every shot is a confession that you screwed up and made too much noise. You learn to love your motion tracker more than your own heartbeat. You learn to throw flares like you’re trying to distract a demon raccoon. You learn fear.

And then there are the Working Joes—androids that were apparently built without the ability to feel emotion or recognize when someone is clearly terrified and hiding under a desk. These rubber-faced maniacs walk like haunted mannequins and talk like your sleep paralysis demon went to customer service school. And when they say, “This could really hurt you,” trust me… they mean it.

Their AI? Questionable. Their vibes? Terrible. Their timing? Impeccably inconvenient.

The story is slow-burn and surprisingly good. It captures the Alien universe perfectly—retro-futurism, corporate greed, eerie silence, and vents. So. Many. Vents. Amanda’s arc is emotional, believable, and makes her one of the most underrated leads in horror gaming. She doesn’t crack jokes. She doesn’t monologue. She just survives. And somehow that’s even more powerful.

Final verdict? Alien: Isolation is survival horror perfection: stressful, atmospheric, infuriating, and deeply immersive. It teaches you the value of silence, the beauty of hiding under a desk, and the raw terror of hearing a tail hit the floor behind you. I crushed on Amanda, feared the alien, hated the androids, and rationed bullets like they were made of platinum.

Would I go back to Sevastopol Station?

No.

But I’ll absolutely hide in a locker and watch someone else suffer again.

199360889.review:
What can I say - it's a very repetitive game that wears out its welcome after about mission 12 (there are 19 missions total).  Everything after that is a bore.  The missions are all pretty much the same game loop - enter a new area, avoid enemies, find a keycard / activate power / etc etc.  The game is basically a series of fetch quests.  It works for a while, before you lose your fear of the enemies, then it's just boring.  There's way too much backtracking, repetition of the same type of mission, and predictable plot points.

On a positive note, the art direction, sounds design, graphical attention to detail, and replication of the Alien universe, are all impeccably done.  As a fan of the Alien franchise I really felt immersed in the world CA built on Sebastopol station.  And the experience did keep me interested for a while during the early and mid game.  That's when it really shines - as the survival horror game its supposed to be.  

However around mission 12 the story just falls apart.  What was a simple and compelling mystery unravels into a superficial action movie, with empty cliffhanger after empty cliffhanger, big explosions that attempt to shock and surprise you but only feel contrived.  That's where the game really falls down (other than the repetitive game mechanics) - the story.  Had the story been kept simple and unique it would have been fine, but the writing falls victims to one of the main pitfalls of the Alien franchise - it tries to include everything in one single storyline.  

In the end it's a decent game that's enjoyable until a point, but has little variety and quality of story after the halfway mark.

199109983.review:
(Spoilers ahead)
Just finished Alien: Isolation, here is my review.
[spoiler] The opening was solid enough to get me invested, but the story quickly starts to feel forced, especially how the station's human groups splinter into random, hostile factions. It’s hard to believe that in a contained disaster like this, so many sides would form. Even in Alien and Aliens, humans stick together under pressure, here, it feels like chaos just for the sake of padding the gameplay. [/spoiler]

[spoiler] The characters are just underwhelming. I didn’t care about Ricardo, Taylor, or most others, their deaths happen without buildup or emotional payoff. Samuels at least had a proper moment. Ripley, the protagonist, isn’t written with much depth. I didn’t really care about her until the very end, when the non-stop danger and collapse around her finally made things tense enough. Until then, she feels like a blank slate.  [/spoiler]

[spoiler] The xenomorph is well-designed and genuinely terrifying. Its AI creates a lot of tension, when you're hiding in a closet and it walks right past, breathing and sniffing near and outside of your hiding spot, it’s quite a scary experience. There's even an option to hold your breath if it gets too close. It can ambush you too, though some of those encounters are clearly scripted. Still, the creature is relentless, to the point of frustration. I played on easy difficulty and still felt like I was barely surviving. Toward the end, I even had to lower it to novice after being stuck for over an hour. You can’t kill it only scare it off, and I get it. The devs clearly wanted to preserve its "dignity", so the player sees it not just as an animal, but as a mindless killing machine, like death itself stalking you. The game could’ve let us kill it in very specific, risky ways without turning the experience into Call of Duty. Instead, it starts to feel repetitive. No matter what gear you get, you're always hiding. But there should have been a better design solution. That constant sense of helplessness wears thin when it never eases up. [/spoiler]

[spoiler]  Crafting and weapons are okay, but most of them don’t help with the real threat, not even a good distraction tools against the xeno. All the guns didn't help at all against the xenomorph. The flamethrower just scares the xenomorph off temporarily, it doesn’t feel like a weapon so much as a water spray bottle for cats. It buys you a few seconds, but that’s it. The rest of your gear is mostly for dealing with humans or androids, who end up feeling like filler enemies compared to the xenomorph. They don’t matter as much because the alien is the real threat (you can flare up and others but the effect is minuscule). So after a while, it feels like you’re collecting tools that don’t actually help with the core problem. [/spoiler]

In short: great atmosphere, great horror tension, but the story and characters are shallow, and the gameplay is fine. A decent experience, but far from perfect. 6/10

199059526.review:
[h1]An Alien: Isolation review from someone who didn’t finish it on PC, but loved it on Steam Deck[/h1]

So I finally finished Alien: Isolation after a 30-hour Hard playthrough on Steam Deck, and I loved it!

Though I'm fairly indifferent to the Alien franchise, this game really sunk its teeth into me. I’ll echo what most people praise about the game: the atmosphere is incredible. The set design nails that gritty, analog sci-fi feel. The sound design and music are top-tier. Every creak and clunk dials the tension way up. Just hearing the Alien skittering in the vents made me slow down and listen. It’s easily one of the more immersive horror games I’ve played.

What surprised me most was how different the game felt on Steam Deck compared to PC. I’d tried it on my main rig before years ago (and even played part of this run on it), but felt less invested. I was always rushing to the next save point, especially if I was under a time crunch. On Steam Deck, being able to suspend and resume whenever I wanted made a huge difference. I slowed down, explored more, and paid more attention to the world. It turned the experience from a “guess I’ll stop here” game into a “just 10 more minutes” one.

That said, it’s not flawless. I think the game overstays its welcome by about 10-15%. By Mission [spoiler]14,[/spoiler] the pacing had noticeably slowed down and the story beats started to feel repetitive and a bit bloated. Because of that, the ending didn’t quite land for me. I liked what played out, but I just wish it had come a few hours earlier to really make that final [spoiler]surprise[/spoiler] hit harder.

The weapon system also left me with mixed thoughts. [spoiler]The revolver and shotgun felt pointless, the bolt gun was useful for exactly one mission, and the flamethrower shows up too early, completely trivializing the Alien. Even on hard, I was constantly maxed out on crafting resources. I mostly relied on the noisemaker and EMP mine, and rarely felt the need to use anything else.[/spoiler]

Also: the lens flares. Seriously, who approved those?

Despite all that though, I’m really glad I came back to this game. It’s not perfect, but it’s a standout horror experience. If for some reason you bounced off it on PC like I did, try it on a handheld. It really does feel like a totally different (and much better) way to play.

199059040.review:
Alien: Isolation. The only Alien game to really understand Alien. And one of the very few things that actually remembered that Alien is supposed to be a HORROR franchise.

Many people praise it, quite a few have their own criticisms of it. The AI is out of this world, the length of the game makes it overstay its welcome. The art direction is unmatched, yet some have issues with the overall gameloop. On and on.

Me? I like the length, I like when an actual good game, a rare gem like this one, isn’t 5 hours long. Too many games I’ve played have so much to offer, but even if they’re still welcome, I see the credits roll.
The AI, as many people have already said, is definitely the reason WHY you’d want to play the game in the first place. Over the years, I grew numb to the horrors of this franchise, I saw the creature ever since I was a little kid, and was not frightened by it any longer. Yet this game made me fear the Xenomorph. This game made me weary of every corner. A horror game that managed to actually be scary. The credit doesn’t just go to the AI itself, but the sound design and the visual quality.

And while the overall gameloop is great, playing hide-and-seek with the Alien is fun, because the levels tend to be quite varied, and you are given a nice few tools to deal with him, so not just hiding under the bed and in a locker, I do kinda wish we’d get to do more, still. Maybe take some cues from older survival horror titles, where I got to revisit locations multiple times, get really accustomed to them.

The story, while well-written and the characters act believably human, do wish was a bit different from the plot of the first movie, this one is kind of a re-tread of it. However, I can forgive such a thing, after all, this is the very first Alien game that actually tried to adapt the first movie into a proper game (those old Commodore 64 games don’t count because they’re just too simple).

Knowing that this game would never get a sequel was depressing. Such a gem left to collect dust as we go back to James Cameron’s interpretation of the franchise, more shooting, more bumrushing Xenos. But some year after I bought the game, we get an announcement of a sequel. A real miracle. Maybe. We’ve yet to see anything about it, the actual people who worked on this game have left Creative Assembly, so who knows what we’ll get in the end. Hope for the best, prepare for the worst.

If you want an actually scary, fun game, you should get it. If you want a proper Alien experience we haven’t gotten in so long, you should get it. If you want a game that was made by the fans for the fans, you should get it. And if you want to get a real gem of art direction, AI design, original score and Giger-esque horrors, you should get it.

199055516.review:
[h1]Alien: Isolation – The True Survival Horror Experience. Score: 95/100[/h1]

I’ve played a lot of horror games, but Alien: Isolation is something else entirely. It doesn’t rely on cheap jump scares or gore. Instead, it slowly wraps you in a blanket of dread and keeps squeezing. The constant fear of the Xenomorph lurking nearby, combined with a beautifully crafted retro-futuristic atmosphere, makes this game feel like a true extension of the original Ridley Scott film. Every sound, flickering light, and shadow keeps you on edge. This isn’t your average horror game, it’s a masterclass in suspense.

[h2]Positives aspects:[/h2]
1. Incredible Atmosphere: The design, lighting, and sound work together to completely immerse you in a terrifying world.

2. Authentic Alien Experience: Feels 100% faithful to the original movie. Every detail screams late '70s sci-fi horror.

3. Smart AI: The Xenomorph isn’t scripted. It learns, adapts, and hunts you dynamically. You never feel safe.

4. Sound Design is God-Tier: Every hiss, creak, or distant thud puts your nerves on edge. Headphones are a must.

5. Rewarding Stealth Gameplay: You’re not a superhero here. Hiding, planning, and timing are everything.

6. Long, Substantial Campaign: Offers a solid length without dragging, especially rare for horror titles.


[h2]Negatives aspects:[/h2]
1. Can Be Too Stressful: It’s so tense that it can get exhausting. Definitely not a chill game.

2. Save System is Brutal: Manual save stations add to the fear but can be frustrating if you die often.

3. Some Pacing Issues: A few late-game sections feel stretched and could have been trimmed.

4: Occasional Bugs: Minor animation or collision issues here and there, though nothing game-breaking.



[h3] My final verdict about Alien: Isolation[/h3] 
This title doesn’t just pay tribute to the Alien franchise, it elevates it. It’s one of the most atmospheric, nerve-wracking survival horror games I’ve ever played. It's not for the faint of heart, but if you want a slow-burn horror experience that respects your intelligence and rewards patience, this is a must-play. This is how you do a licensed game right. Pure tension, pure Alien.

198828821.review:
Alien: Isolation is my favorite horror game of all time. I’ve played it on multiple platforms, and I own it across more PC storefronts than I care to admit, Steam, GOG, Epic... if there’s a launcher that sells it, chances are I bought it. And I regret nothing. Also it runs great on Switch.

This isn’t just a horror game. It’s a survival experience. Every creak of the ship, every flicker of the lights, every hiss from a nearby vent feels like a threat. And that’s because it is. The Alien doesn’t follow scripts. It doesn’t wait for you to finish looting a locker. It hunts, it adapts, and it learns. It is, without exaggeration, one of the best AI implementations in gaming. A relentless, unpredictable, perfect nightmare.

Amanda Ripley is a great protagonist, smart, resourceful, and very aware she’s constantly one bad breath away from death. The setting captures the original Alien film’s aesthetic perfectly: retro-futuristic terminals, clunky tech, claustrophobic hallways drenched in fear. It doesn’t just pay homage, it lives in that world.

Alien: Isolation respects your intelligence and punishes your arrogance. And somehow, you keep crawling back. If you want real tension, real fear, and a masterclass in environmental storytelling, this is the one.

First the amazing part: the graphics hold up extremely well. It's a testament to the vision of the creative team behind the first Alien movie that the design and aesthetics still feel extremely well done so many years later, but even the team behind the game did a great job with it.

The Alien is legitimately scary and can keep you on your toes for hours. But here comes the issue. The first handful of hours is great, but then it becomes apparent that you're mostly running in circles in the same enviroments, and the puzzles never progress or scale up to match your relatively higher understanding of the game's mechanics. The first time I had to deal with the Xenomorph I was scared but thrilled. By the last three or so hours I was bored and every death felt lame because it was dragged well beyond the point where it was still enjoyable. 

A good 30% of your playtime is inflated because you have to crouch for a lot of sections which is fine but by the end I was so frustrated at the slow pace that I started to play more aggresively just to get it over with.
